‚ÄĘ REFERENCES

Straightforward parse_name examples are the chess pieces object and the kittens class of
‚ÄėAlice Through the Looking-Glass‚Äô. Lengthier ones are found in ‚ÄėBalances‚Äô, especially in
the white cubes class. ‚ÄĘMiron Schmidt‚Äôs library extension "calyx_adjectives.h",
based on earlier work by Andrew Clover, provides for objects to have ‚Äė‚Äėadnames‚Äô‚Äô as
well as ‚Äė‚Äėnames‚Äô‚Äô: ‚Äė‚Äėadnames‚Äô‚Äô are usually adjectives, and are regarded as being less good

Ôú≤ÔúĪÔúį

¬ßÔú≤Ôúł ÔĚ®ÔĚĮÔĚ∑ ÔĚģÔĚĮÔĚĶÔĚģÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ§
matches for an object than ‚Äė‚Äėnames‚Äô‚Äô. In this system ‚Äė‚Äėget string‚Äô‚Äô would take either a
string bag or a ball of string, but if both were present would take the ball of string,
because ‚Äė‚Äėstring‚Äô‚Äô is in that case a noun rather than an adjective.

Ôú≤ÔúĪÔúĪ

¬ß29

Plural names for duplicated objects

A notorious challenge for adventure game parsers is to handle a
collection of, say, ten gold coins, allowing the player to use them
independently of each other, while gathering them together into
groups in descriptions and inventories. Two problems must be
overcome: firstly, the game has to be able to talk to the player in plurals, and
secondly vice versa. First, then, game to player:
Class GoldCoin
with name ‚Äôgold‚Äô ‚Äôcoin‚Äô,
short_name "gold coin",
plural "gold coins";

(and then similar silver and bronze coin classes)
Object bag "bag"
with name ‚Äôbag‚Äô,
has container open openable;
GoldCoin ->;
GoldCoin ->;
GoldCoin ->;
SilverCoin ->;
SilverCoin ->;
BronzeCoin ->;

Now we have a bag of six coins. The player looking inside the bag will get
>look inside bag
In the bag are three gold coins, two silver coins and a bronze coin.

How does the library know that the three gold coins are the same as each
other, but the others different? It doesn‚Äôt look at the classes but the names. It
will only group together things which:
(a) have a plural set, and
(b) are ‚Äė‚Äėindistinguishable‚Äô‚Äô from each other.
‚Äė‚ÄėIndistinguishable‚Äô‚Äô means they have the same name words as each other,
possibly in a different order, so that nothing the player can type will separate
the two.
Ôú≤ÔúĪÔú≤

¬ßÔú≤ÔúĻ ÔĚįÔĚ¨ÔĚĶÔĚ≤ÔĚ°ÔĚ¨ ÔĚģÔĚ°ÔĚ≠ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ ÔĚ¶ÔĚĮÔĚ≤ ÔĚ§ÔĚĶÔĚįÔĚ¨ÔĚ©ÔĚ£ÔĚ°ÔĚīÔĚ•ÔĚ§ ÔĚĮÔĚĘÔĚ™ÔĚ•ÔĚ£ÔĚīÔĚ≥
4

Actually, it‚Äôs a little more subtle than this. What it groups together depends
slightly on the context of the list being written. When it‚Äôs writing a list which prints out
details of which objects are providing light, for instance (as an inventory does), it won‚Äôt
group together two objects if one is lit but the other isn‚Äôt. Similarly for objects with
visible possessions or which can be worn.

44 This ramifies further when the objects have a parse_name routine supplied. If they

have different parse_name routines, the library decides that they are distinguishable.
But if they have the same parse_name routine, for instance by inheriting it from a class
definition, then the library has no alternative but to ask them. What happens is that:
(1) A variable called parser_action is set to the special value ##TheSame, a value it
never has at any other time;
(2) Two variables, called parser_one and parser_two are set to the two objects in
question;
(3) Their parse_name routine is called. If it returns:
‚ąí1 the objects are declared ‚Äė‚Äėindistinguishable";
‚ąí2 they are declared different.
(4) Otherwise, the usual rules apply and the library looks at the ordinary name fields
of the objects.

44 You may even want to provide a parse_name routine for objects which otherwise
don‚Äôt need one, just to speed up the process of the library telling if two objects are
distinguishable ‚Äď if there were 30 gold coins in one place the parser would be doing a
lot of work comparing names, but you can make the decision much faster.
‚ÄĘ 44EXERCISE 79

Perhaps the neatest trick of parsing in any Infocom game occurs in ‚ÄėSpellbreaker‚Äô,
which has a set of white cubes which are indistinguishable until the player writes words
onto them with a magic burin (a medieval kind of pen), after which it‚Äôs possible to tell
them apart. Imitate this in Inform.

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

Secondly, the player talking to the computer. Suppose a game involves
collecting a number of similar items, such as a set of nine crowns in different
colours. Then you‚Äôd want the parser to recognise things like:
>drop all of the crowns except green
>drop the three other crowns

Putting the word ‚Äôcrowns‚Äô in the name lists of the crown objects is not quite
right, because the parser will still think that ‚Äė‚Äėcrowns‚Äô‚Äô might refer to a single
specific item. Instead, put in the word ‚Äôcrowns//p‚Äô. The suffix //p marks out
the dictionary word ‚Äė‚Äėcrowns‚Äô‚Äô as one that can refer to more than one game
object at once. (So that you shouldn‚Äôt set this for the word ‚Äė‚Äėgrapes‚Äô‚Äô if a bunch
Ôú≤ÔúĪÔú≥

¬ßÔú≤ÔúĻ ÔĚįÔĚ¨ÔĚĶÔĚ≤ÔĚ°ÔĚ¨ ÔĚģÔĚ°ÔĚ≠ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ ÔĚ¶ÔĚĮÔĚ≤ ÔĚ§ÔĚĶÔĚįÔĚ¨ÔĚ©ÔĚ£ÔĚ°ÔĚīÔĚ•ÔĚ§ ÔĚĮÔĚĘÔĚ™ÔĚ•ÔĚ£ÔĚīÔĚ≥
of grapes is a single game object; you should give that object the pluralname
attribute instead, as in ¬ß26 back at the start of this chapter.) For example the
GoldCoin class would read:
Class GoldCoin
with name ‚Äôgold‚Äô ‚Äôcoin‚Äô ‚Äôcoins//p‚Äô,
short_name "gold coin",
plural "gold coins";

Now when the player types ‚Äė‚Äėtake coins‚Äô‚Äô, the parser interprets this as ‚Äė‚Äėtake all
the coins within reach‚Äô‚Äô.
44 The only snag is that now the word ‚Äôcoins‚Äô is marked as //p everywhere in the
game, in all circumstances. Here is a more complicated way to achieve the same result,
but strictly in context of these objects alone. We need to make the parse_name routine
tell the parser that yes, there was a match, but that it was a plural. The way to do this
is to set parser_action to ##PluralFound, another special value. So, for example:
Class Crown
with parse_name [ i j;
for (::) {
j = NextWord();
if (j == ‚Äôcrown‚Äô or self.name) i++;
else {
if (j == ‚Äôcrowns‚Äô) {
parser_action = ##PluralFound; i++;
}
else return i;
}
}
];
This code assumes that the crown objects have just one name each, their colours.

‚ÄĘ EXERCISE 80

Write a ‚Äėcherub‚Äô class so that if the player tries to call them ‚Äė‚Äėcherubs", a message like
‚Äė‚ÄėI‚Äôll let this go once, but the plural of cherub is cherubim" appears.

Ôú≤ÔúĪÔúī

¬ß30

How verbs are parsed
‚Äė‚Äė. . .I can see that the grammar gets tucked into the tales and poetry
as one gives pills in jelly.‚Äô‚Äô
‚ÄĒ Louisa May Alcott (1832‚Äď1888), Little Women

Here is how the parser reads in a whole command. Given a stream
of text like
saint / peter / , / take / the / keys / from / paul
it first breaks it into words, as shown, and then calls the entry point routine
BeforeParsing (which you can provide, if you want to, in order to meddle
with the text stream before parsing gets underway). The parser then works
out who is being addressed, if anyone, by looking for a comma, and trying out
the text up to there as a noun matching an animate or talkable object: in
this case St Peter. This person is called the ‚Äė‚Äėactor‚Äô‚Äô, since he or she is going
to perform the action, and is most often the player (thus, typing ‚Äė‚Äėmyself, go
north‚Äô‚Äô is equivalent to typing ‚Äė‚Äėgo north‚Äô‚Äô). The next word, in this case ‚Äôtake‚Äô,
is the ‚Äė‚Äėverb word‚Äô‚Äô. An Inform verb usually has several English verb words
attached, which are called synonyms of each other: for instance, the library is
set up with
‚Äė‚Äėtake‚Äô‚Äô = ‚Äė‚Äėcarry‚Äô‚Äô = ‚Äė‚Äėhold‚Äô‚Äô
all referring to the same Inform verb.
4

The parser sets up variables actor and verb_word while working. (In the example
above, their values would be the St Peter object and ‚Äôtake‚Äô, respectively.)

4

This brief discussion is simplified in two ways. Firstly, it leaves out directions,
because Inform considers that the name of a direction-object implies ‚Äė‚Äėgo‚Äô‚Äô: thus
‚Äė‚Äėnorth‚Äô‚Äô means ‚Äė‚Äėgo north‚Äô‚Äô. Secondly, it misses out the grammar property described in
¬ß18, which can cause different actors to recognise different grammars.

‚ÄĘ 4EXERCISE 81

Use BeforeParsing to implement a lamp which, when rubbed, produces a genie who
casts a spell to make the player confuse the words ‚Äė‚Äėwhite‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėblack‚Äô‚Äô.

¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑
Ôú≤ÔúĪÔúĶ

¬ßÔú≥Ôúį ÔĚ®ÔĚĮÔĚ∑ ÔĚ∂ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ÔĚĘÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ§
This section is about verbs, which are defined with ‚Äė‚Äėgrammar‚Äô‚Äô, meaning usages
of the directives Verb and Extend. The library contains a substantial amount
of grammar as it is, and this forms (most of) the library file "Grammar.h".
Grammar defined in your own code can either build on this or selectively knock
it down, but either way it should be made after the inclusion of "Grammar.h".
For instance, making a new synonym for an existing verb is easy:
Verb ‚Äôsteal‚Äô ‚Äôacquire‚Äô ‚Äôgrab‚Äô = ‚Äôtake‚Äô;

Now ‚Äė‚Äėsteal‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėacquire‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėgrab‚Äô‚Äô are synonyms for ‚Äė‚Äėtake‚Äô‚Äô.
4

One can also prise synonyms apart, as will appear later.

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

To return to the text above, the parser has now recognised the English word
‚Äė‚Äėtake‚Äô‚Äô as one of those which can refer to a particular Inform verb. It has
reached word 5 and still has ‚Äė‚Äėthe keys from paul‚Äô‚Äô left to understand.
Every Inform verb has a ‚Äė‚Äėgrammar‚Äô‚Äô which consists of a list of one or
more ‚Äė‚Äėgrammar lines‚Äô‚Äô, each of them a pattern which the rest of the text might
match. The parser tries the first, then the second and so on, and accepts the
earliest one that matches, without ever considering later ones.
A line is a row of ‚Äė‚Äėtokens‚Äô‚Äô. Typical tokens might mean ‚Äėthe name of a
nearby object‚Äô, ‚Äėthe word ‚Äôfrom‚Äô‚Äô or ‚Äėsomebody‚Äôs name‚Äô. To match a line, the
parser must match against each token in sequence. Continuing the example,
the parser accepts the line of three tokens
hone or more nounsi hthe word fromi ha nouni
as matching ‚Äė‚Äėthe keys from paul‚Äô‚Äô.
Every grammar line has the name of an action attached, and in this case it
is Remove: so the parser has ground up the original text into just four quantities,
ending up with
actor = StPeter

action = Remove

noun = gold_keys

second = StPaul

The parser‚Äôs job is now complete, and the rest of the Inform library can get
on with processing the action or, as in this case, an order being addressed to
somebody other than the player.
4

The action for the line currently being worked through is stored in the variable
action_to_be; or, at earlier stages when the verb hasn‚Äôt been deciphered yet, it holds
the value NULL.

Ôú≤ÔúĪÔú∂

¬ßÔú≥Ôúį ÔĚ®ÔĚĮÔĚ∑ ÔĚ∂ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ÔĚĘÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ§
¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

The Verb directive creates Inform verbs, giving them some English verb words
and a grammar. The library‚Äôs "Grammar.h" file consists almost exclusively of
Verb directives: here is an example simplified from one of them.
Verb ‚Äôtake‚Äô ‚Äôget‚Äô ‚Äôcarry‚Äô ‚Äôhold‚Äô
* ‚Äôout‚Äô
-> Exit
* multi
-> Take
* multiinside ‚Äôfrom‚Äô noun -> Remove
* ‚Äôin‚Äô noun
-> Enter
* multiinside ‚Äôoff‚Äô noun -> Remove
* ‚Äôoff‚Äô held
-> Disrobe
* ‚Äôinventory‚Äô
-> Inv;

(You can look at the grammar being used in a game with the debugging verb
‚Äė‚Äėshowverb‚Äô‚Äô: see ¬ß7.) Each line of grammar begins with a *, gives a list of
tokens as far as -> and then the action which the line produces. The first
line can only be matched by something like ‚Äė‚Äėget out‚Äô‚Äô, the second might be
matched by
‚Äė‚Äėtake the banana‚Äô‚Äô
‚Äė‚Äėget all the fruit except the apple‚Äô‚Äô
and so on. A full list of tokens will be given later: briefly, ‚Äôout‚Äô means the
literal word ‚Äė‚Äėout‚Äô‚Äô, multi means one or more objects nearby, noun means
just one and multiinside means one or more objects inside the second
noun. In this book, grammar tokens are written in the style noun to prevent
confusion (as there is also a variable called noun).
4

Some verbs are marked as meta ‚Äď these are the verbs leading to Group 1 actions,
those which are not really part of the game‚Äôs world: for example, ‚Äė‚Äėsave‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėscore‚Äô‚Äô and
‚Äė‚Äėquit‚Äô‚Äô. For example:
Verb meta ‚Äôscore‚Äô * -> Score;
and any debugging verbs you create would probably work better this way, since
meta-verbs are protected from interference by the game and take up no game time.

¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

After the -> in each line is the name of an action. Giving a name in this way is
what creates an action, and if you give the name of one which doesn‚Äôt already
Ôú≤ÔúĪÔú∑

¬ßÔú≥Ôúį ÔĚ®ÔĚĮÔĚ∑ ÔĚ∂ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ÔĚĘÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ§
exist then you must also write a routine to execute the action, even if it‚Äôs one
which doesn‚Äôt do very much. The name of the routine is always the name of
the action with Sub appended. For instance:
[ XyzzySub; "Nothing happens."; ];
Verb ‚Äôxyzzy‚Äô * -> Xyzzy;

will make a new magic-word verb ‚Äė‚Äėxyzzy‚Äô‚Äô, which always says ‚Äė‚ÄėNothing
happens‚Äô‚Äô ‚Äď always, that is, unless some before rule gets there first, as it might
do in certain magic places. Xyzzy is now an action just as good as all the
standard ones: ##Xyzzy gives its action number, and you can write before
rules for it in Xyzzy: fields just as you would for, say, Take.
4 Finally, the line can end with the word reverse. This is only useful if there are
objects or numbers in the line which occur in the wrong order. An example from the
library‚Äôs grammar:
Verb ‚Äôshow‚Äô ‚Äôpresent‚Äô ‚Äôdisplay‚Äô
* creature held
-> Show reverse
* held ‚Äôto‚Äô creature -> Show;
The point is that the Show action expects the first parameter to be an item, and the
second to be a person. When the text ‚Äė‚Äėshow him the shield‚Äô‚Äô is typed in, the parser
must reverse the two parameters ‚Äė‚Äėhim‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėthe shield‚Äô‚Äô before causing a Show action.
On the other hand, in ‚Äė‚Äėshow the shield to him‚Äô‚Äô the parameters are in the right order
already.

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

The library defines grammars for the 100 or so English verbs most often used
by adventure games. However, in practice you quite often need to alter these,
usually to add extra lines of grammar but sometimes to remove existing ones.
For instance, suppose you would like ‚Äė‚Äėdrop charges‚Äô‚Äô to be a command in a
detection game (or a naval warfare game). This means adding a new grammar
line to the ‚Äė‚Äėdrop‚Äô‚Äô verb. The Extend directive is provided for exactly this
purpose:
Extend ‚Äôdrop‚Äô * ‚Äôcharges‚Äô -> DropCharges;

Normally, extra lines of grammar are added at the bottom of those already
there, so that this will be the very last grammar line tested by the parser. This
may not be what you want. For instance, ‚Äė‚Äėtake‚Äô‚Äô has a grammar line reading
* multi -> Take

Ôú≤ÔúĪÔúł

¬ßÔú≥Ôúį ÔĚ®ÔĚĮÔĚ∑ ÔĚ∂ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ÔĚĘÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ§
quite early on. So if you want to add a grammar line diverting ‚Äė‚Äėtake hfoodi" to
a different action, like so:
* edible -> Eat

( edible being a token matching anything which has the attribute edible)
then it‚Äôs no good adding this at the bottom of the Take grammar, because the
earlier line will always be matched first. What you need is for the new line to
go in at the top, not the bottom:
Extend ‚Äôtake‚Äô first
* edible -> Eat;

You might even want to throw away the old grammar completely, not just add
a line or two. For this, use
Extend ‚Äôpress‚Äô replace
* ‚Äôcharges‚Äô -> PressCharges;

and now the verb ‚Äė‚Äėpress‚Äô‚Äô has no other sense but this, and can‚Äôt be used in the
sense of pressing down on objects any more, because those grammar lines are
gone. To sum up, Extend can optionally take one of three keywords:
replace
first
last

replace the old grammar with this one;
insert the new grammar at the top;
insert the new grammar at the bottom;

with last being the default.
4

In library grammar, some verbs have many synonyms: for instance,
‚Äôattack‚Äô ‚Äôbreak‚Äô ‚Äôsmash‚Äô ‚Äôhit‚Äô ‚Äôfight‚Äô ‚Äôwreck‚Äô ‚Äôcrack‚Äô
‚Äôdestroy‚Äô ‚Äômurder‚Äô ‚Äôkill‚Äô ‚Äôtorture‚Äô ‚Äôpunch‚Äô ‚Äôthump‚Äô

are all treated as identical. But you might want to distinguish between murder and
lesser crimes. For this, try
Extend only ‚Äômurder‚Äô ‚Äôkill‚Äô replace
* animate -> Murder;
The keyword only tells Inform to extract the two verbs ‚Äė‚Äėmurder" and ‚Äė‚Äėkill". These then
become a new verb which is initially an identical copy of the old one, but then replace
tells Inform to throw that away in favour of an entirely new grammar. Similarly,
Extend only ‚Äôrun‚Äô * ‚Äôprogram‚Äô -> Compute;
makes ‚Äė‚Äėrun" behave exactly like ‚Äė‚Äėgo" and ‚Äė‚Äėwalk‚Äô‚Äô, as these three words are ordinarily
synonymous to the library, except that it also recognises ‚Äė‚Äėprogram", so that ‚Äė‚Äėrun
program" activates a computer but ‚Äė‚Äėwalk program" doesn‚Äôt. Other good pairs to
separate might be ‚Äė‚Äėcross‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėenter‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėdrop‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėthrow‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėgive‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėfeed‚Äô‚Äô,
‚Äė‚Äėswim‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėdive‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėkiss‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėhug‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėcut‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėprune‚Äô‚Äô. Bear in mind that once a
pair has been split apart like this, any subsequent change to one will not also change
the other.

Ôú≤ÔúĪÔúĻ

¬ßÔú≥Ôúį ÔĚ®ÔĚĮÔĚ∑ ÔĚ∂ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ÔĚĘÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ§
44 Occasionally verb definition commands are not enough. For example, in the

original ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô, the player could type the name of an adjacent place which had
previously been visited, and be taken there. (This feature isn‚Äôt included in the Inform
example version of the game in order to keep the source code as simple as possible.)
There are several laborious ways to code this, but here‚Äôs a concise way. The library
calls the UnknownVerb entry point routine (if you provide one) when the parser can‚Äôt
even get past the first word. This has two options: it can return false, in which case
the parser just goes on to complain as it would have done anyway. Otherwise, it can
return a verb word which is substituted for what the player actually typed. Here is one
way the ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô room-naming might work. Suppose that every room has been given
a property called go_verb listing the words which refer to it, so for instance the well
house might be defined along these lines:
AboveGround Inside_Building "Inside Building"
with description
"You are inside a building, a well house for a
large spring.",
go_verb ‚Äôwell‚Äô ‚Äôhouse‚Äô ‚Äôinside‚Äô ‚Äôbuilding‚Äô,
...
The UnknownVerb routine then looks through the possible compass directions for
already-visited rooms, checking against words stored in this new property:
Global go_verb_direction;
[ UnknownVerb word room direction adjacent;
room = real_location;
objectloop (direction in compass) {
adjacent = room.(direction.door_dir);
if (adjacent ofclass Object && adjacent has visited
&& adjacent provides go_verb
&& WordInProperty(word, adjacent, go_verb)) {
go_verb_direction = direction;
return ‚Äôgo.verb‚Äô;
}
}
if (room provides go_verb
&& WordInProperty(word, room, go_verb)) {
go_verb_direction = "You‚Äôre already there!";
return ‚Äôgo.verb‚Äô;
}
objectloop (room provides go_verb && room has visited
&& WordInProperty(word, room, go_verb)) {
go_verb_direction = "You can‚Äôt get there from here!";
return ‚Äôgo.verb‚Äô;
}

Ôú≤Ôú≤Ôúį

¬ßÔú≥Ôúį ÔĚ®ÔĚĮÔĚ∑ ÔĚ∂ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ÔĚĘÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ§
objectloop (room provides go_verb && room hasnt visited
&& WordInProperty(word, room, go_verb)) {
go_verb_direction = "But you don‚Äôt know the way there!";
return ‚Äôgo.verb‚Äô;
}
rfalse;
];
When successful, this routine stores either a compass direction (an object belonging to
the compass) in the variable go_verb_direction, or else a string to print. (Note that
an UnknownVerb routine shouldn‚Äôt print anything itself, as this might be inappropriate
in view of subsequent parsing, or if the actor isn‚Äôt the player.) The routine then tells the
parser to treat the verb as if it were ‚Äôgo.verb‚Äô, and as this doesn‚Äôt exist yet, we must
define it:
[ Go_VerbSub;
if (go_verb_direction ofclass String)
print_ret (string) go_verb_direction;
<<Go go_verb_direction>>;
];
Verb ‚Äôgo.verb‚Äô * -> Go_Verb;

‚ÄĘ 44EXERCISE 82

A minor deficiency with the above system is that the parser may print out strange
responses like ‚Äė‚ÄėI only understood you as far as wanting to go.verb.‚Äô‚Äô if the player types
something odd like ‚Äė‚Äėbedquilt the nugget‚Äô‚Äô. How can we ensure that the parser will
always say something like ‚Äė‚ÄėI only understood you as far as wanting to go to Bedquilt.‚Äô‚Äô?

‚ÄĘ REFERENCES

‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô makes a string of simple Verb definitions; ‚ÄėAlice Through the LookingGlass‚Äô uses Extend a little.
‚ÄĘ‚ÄėBalances‚Äô has a large extra grammar and also uses
the UnknownVerb and PrintVerb entry points. ‚ÄĘIrene Callaci‚Äôs "AskTellOrder.h"
library extension file makes an elegant use of BeforeParsing to convert commands in
the form ‚Äė‚Äėask mr darcy to dance‚Äô‚Äô or ‚Äė‚Äėtell jack to go north‚Äô‚Äô to Inform‚Äôs preferred form
‚Äė‚Äėmr darcy, dance‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėjack, go north‚Äô‚Äô.

Ôú≤Ôú≤ÔúĪ

¬ß31

Tokens of grammar
The complete list of grammar tokens is given in the table below. These tokens are all described in this section except for
scope = hRoutinei , which is postponed to the next.
‚Äôhwordi‚Äô

that literal word only

noun

any object in scope

held

object held by the actor

multi

one or more objects in scope

multiheld

one or more held objects

multiexcept

one or more in scope, except the other object

multiinside

one or more in scope, inside the other object

hattributei

any object in scope which has the attribute

creature

an object in scope which is animate

noun = hRoutinei

any object in scope passing the given test

scope = hRoutinei

an object in this definition of scope

number

a number only

hRoutinei

any text accepted by the given routine

topic

any text at all

To recap, the parser goes through a line of grammar tokens trying to match
each against some text from the player‚Äôs input. Each token that matches must
produce one of the following five results:
(a) a single object;
(b) a ‚Äė‚Äėmultiple object‚Äô‚Äô, that is, a set of objects;
(c) a number;
(d) a ‚Äė‚Äėconsultation topic‚Äô‚Äô, that is, a collection of words left unparsed to be
looked through later;
(e) no information at all.
Ordinarily, a single line, though it may contain many tokens, can produce at
most two substantial results ((a) to (d)), at most one of which can be multiple
Ôú≤Ôú≤Ôú≤

¬ßÔú≥ÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚĮÔĚęÔĚ•ÔĚģÔĚ≥ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚßÔĚ≤ÔĚ°ÔĚ≠ÔĚ≠ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤
(b). (See the exercises below if this is a problem.) For instance, suppose the
text ‚Äė‚Äėgreen apple on the table‚Äô‚Äô is parsed against the grammar line:
* multi ‚Äôon‚Äô noun -> Insert

The multi token matches ‚Äė‚Äėgreen apple‚Äô‚Äô (result: a single object, since
although multi can match a multiple object, it doesn‚Äôt have to), ‚Äôon‚Äô
matches ‚Äė‚Äėon‚Äô‚Äô (result: nothing) and the second noun token matches ‚Äė‚Äėthe
table‚Äô‚Äô (result: a single object again). There are two substantial results, both
objects, so the action that comes out is <Insert apple table>. If the text
had been ‚Äė‚Äėall the fruit on the table‚Äô‚Äô, the multi token might have resulted in
a list: perhaps of an apple, an orange and a pear. The parser would then have
generated and run through three actions in turn: <Insert apple table>, then
<Insert orange table> and finally <Insert pear table>, printing out the
name of each item and a colon before running the action:
>put all the fruit on the table
Cox‚Äôs pippin: Done.
orange: Done.
Conference pear: Done.

The library‚Äôs routine InsertSub, which actually handles the action, only deals
with single objects at a time, and in each case it printed ‚Äė‚ÄėDone.‚Äô‚Äô
¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

‚Äôhwordi‚Äô
This matches only the literal word given, sometimes called a
preposition because it usually is one, and produces no resulting information.
(There can therefore be as many or as few of them on a grammar line as
desired.) It often happens that several prepositions really mean the same thing
for a given verb: for instance ‚Äė‚Äėin‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėinto‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėinside‚Äô‚Äô are often synonymous.
As a convenient shorthand, then, you can write a series of prepositions (only)
with slashes / in between, to mean ‚Äė‚Äėone of these words‚Äô‚Äô. For example:
* noun ‚Äôin‚Äô/‚Äôinto‚Äô/‚Äôinside‚Äô noun -> Insert

noun
Matches any single object ‚Äė‚Äėin scope‚Äô‚Äô, a term defined in the next
section and which roughly means ‚Äė‚Äėvisible to the player at the moment‚Äô‚Äô.
held
Matches any single object which is an immediate possession of the
actor. (Thus, if a key is inside a box being carried by the actor, the box
might match but the key cannot.) This is convenient for two reasons. Firstly,
Ôú≤Ôú≤Ôú≥

¬ßÔú≥ÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚĮÔĚęÔĚ•ÔĚģÔĚ≥ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚßÔĚ≤ÔĚ°ÔĚ≠ÔĚ≠ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤
many actions, such as Eat or Wear, only sensibly apply to things being held.
Secondly, suppose we have grammar
Verb ‚Äôeat‚Äô * held -> Eat;

and the player types ‚Äė‚Äėeat the banana‚Äô‚Äô while the banana is, say, in plain view
on a shelf. It would be petty of the game to refuse on the grounds that the
banana is not being held. So the parser will generate a Take action for the
banana and then, if the Take action succeeds, an Eat action. Notice that the
parser does not just pick up the object, but issues an action in the proper way ‚Äď
so if the banana had rules making it too slippery to pick up, it won‚Äôt be picked
up. This is called ‚Äė‚Äėimplicit taking‚Äô‚Äô, and happens only for the player, not for
other actors.
multi
Matches one or more objects in scope. The multi- tokens
indicate that a list of one or more objects can go here. The parser works out
all the things the player has asked for, sorting out plural nouns and words like
‚Äė‚Äėexcept" in the process. For instance, ‚Äė‚Äėall the apples‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėthe duck and the
drake‚Äô‚Äô could match a multi token but not a noun token.
multiexcept
Matches one or more objects in scope, except that it does
not match the other single object parsed in the same grammar line. This is
provided to make commands like ‚Äė‚Äėput everything in the rucksack‚Äô‚Äô come out
right: the ‚Äė‚Äėeverything‚Äô‚Äô is matched by all of the player‚Äôs possessions except the
rucksack, which stops the parser from generating an action to put the rucksack
inside itself.
multiinside
Similarly, this matches anything inside the other single
object parsed on the same grammar line, which is good for parsing commands
like ‚Äė‚Äėremove everything from the cupboard‚Äô‚Äô.
hattributei
Matches any object in scope which has the given attribute.
This is useful for sorting out actions according to context, and perhaps the
ultimate example might be an old-fashioned ‚Äė‚Äėuse‚Äô‚Äô verb:
Verb ‚Äôuse‚Äô ‚Äôemploy‚Äô ‚Äôutilise‚Äô
* edible
-> Eat
* clothing -> Wear
...
* enterable -> Enter;

Ôú≤Ôú≤Ôúī

¬ßÔú≥ÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚĮÔĚęÔĚ•ÔĚģÔĚ≥ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚßÔĚ≤ÔĚ°ÔĚ≠ÔĚ≠ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤

creature
Matches any object in scope which behaves as if living. This
normally means having animate: but, as an exceptional rule, if the action on
the grammar line is Ask, Answer, Tell or AskFor then having talkable is also
acceptable.
noun = hRoutinei
‚Äė‚ÄėAny single object in scope satisfying some condition‚Äô‚Äô. When determining whether an object passes this test, the parser sets
the variable noun to the object in question and calls the routine. If it returns
true, the parser accepts the object, and otherwise it rejects it. For example,
the following should only apply to animals kept in a cage:
[ CagedCreature;
if (noun in wicker_cage) rtrue; rfalse;
];
Verb ‚Äôfree‚Äô ‚Äôrelease‚Äô
* noun=CagedCreature -> FreeAnimal;

So that only nouns which pass the CagedCreature test are allowed. The
CagedCreature routine can appear anywhere in the source code, though it‚Äôs
tidier to keep it nearby.
scope = hRoutinei
An even more powerful token, which means ‚Äė‚Äėan
object in scope‚Äô‚Äô where scope is redefined specially. You can also choose
whether or not it can accept a multiple object. See ¬ß32.
number
Matches any decimal number from 0 upwards (though it rounds
off large numbers to 10,000), and also matches the numbers ‚Äė‚Äėone‚Äô‚Äô to ‚Äė‚Äėtwenty‚Äô‚Äô
written in English. For example:
Verb ‚Äôtype‚Äô * number -> TypeNum;

causes actions like <Typenum 504> when the player types ‚Äė‚Äėtype 504‚Äô‚Äô. Note
that noun is set to 504, not to an object. (While inp1 is set to 1, indicating
that this ‚Äė‚Äėfirst input‚Äô‚Äô is intended as a number: if the noun had been the object
which happened to have number 504, then inp1 would have been set to this
object, the same as noun.) If you need more exact number parsing, without
rounding off, and including negative numbers, see the exercise below.
Ôú≤Ôú≤ÔúĶ

¬ßÔú≥ÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚĮÔĚęÔĚ•ÔĚģÔĚ≥ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚßÔĚ≤ÔĚ°ÔĚ≠ÔĚ≠ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤
‚ÄĘ EXERCISE 83

Some games, such as David M. Baggett‚Äôs game ‚ÄėThe Legend Lives!‚Äô produce footnotes
every now and then. Arrange matters so that these are numbered [1], [2] and so on in
order of appearance, to be read by the player when ‚Äė‚Äėfootnote 1‚Äô‚Äô is typed.

4

The entry point ParseNumber allows you to provide your own number-parsing
routine, which opens up many sneaky possibilities ‚Äď Roman numerals, coordinates like
‚Äė‚ÄėJ4‚Äô‚Äô, very long telephone numbers and so on. This takes the form
[ ParseNumber buffer length;
...returning false if no match is made, or the number otherwise...
];

and examines the supposed ‚Äėnumber‚Äô held at the byte address buffer, a row of
characters of the given length. If you provide a ParseNumber routine but return false
from it, then the parser falls back on its usual number-parsing mechanism to see if that
does any better.

44 Note that ParseNumber can‚Äôt return 0 to mean the number zero, because 0 is the
same as false. Probably ‚Äė‚Äėzero‚Äô‚Äô won‚Äôt be needed too often, but if it is you can always
return some value like 1000 and code the verb in question to understand this as 0.
(Sorry: this was a poor design decision made too long ago to change now.)
topic
This token matches as much text as possible, regardless of what it
says, producing no result. As much text as possible means ‚Äė‚Äėuntil the end of the
typing, or, if the next token is a preposition, until that preposition is reached‚Äô‚Äô.
The only way this can fail is if it finds no text at all. Otherwise, the variable
consult_from is set to the number of the first word of the matched text and
consult_words to the number of words. See ¬ß16 and ¬ß18 for examples of
topics being used.
hRoutinei
The most flexible token is simply the name of a ‚Äė‚Äėgeneral
parsing routine‚Äô‚Äô. As the name suggests, it is a routine to do some parsing
which can have any outcome you choose, and many of the interesting things
you can do with the parser involve writing one. A general parsing routine looks
at the word stream using NextWord and wn (see ¬ß28) to make its decisions, and
should return one of the following. Note that the values beginning GPR_ are
constants defined by the library.
GPR_FAIL
GPR_MULTIPLE
GPR_NUMBER
GPR_PREPOSITION
Ôú≤Ôú≤Ôú∂

if there is no match;
if the result is a multiple object;
if the result is a number;
if there is a match but no result;

¬ßÔú≥ÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚĮÔĚęÔĚ•ÔĚģÔĚ≥ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚßÔĚ≤ÔĚ°ÔĚ≠ÔĚ≠ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤
GPR_REPARSE
O

to reparse the whole command from scratch; or
if the result is a single object O.

On an unsuccessful match, returning GPR_FAIL, it doesn‚Äôt matter what the
final value of wn is. On a successful match it should be left pointing to the next
thing after what the routine understood. Since NextWord moves wn on by one
each time it is called, this happens automatically unless the routine has read
too far. For example:
[ OnAtorIn;
if (NextWord() == ‚Äôon‚Äô or ‚Äôat‚Äô or ‚Äôin‚Äô) return GPR_PREPOSITION;
return GPR_FAIL;
];

duplicates the effect of ‚Äôon‚Äô/‚Äôat‚Äô/‚Äôin‚Äô , that is, it makes a token which
accepts any of the words ‚Äė‚Äėon", ‚Äė‚Äėat" or ‚Äė‚Äėin" as prepositions. Similarly,
[ Anything;
while (NextWordStopped() ~= -1) ; return GPR_PREPOSITION;
];

accepts the entire rest of the line (even an empty text, if there are no more
words on the line), ignoring it. NextWordStopped is a form of NextWord which
returns the special value ‚ąí1 once the original word stream has run out.
If you return GPR_NUMBER, the number which you want to be the result
should be put into the library‚Äôs variable parsed_number.
If you return GPR_MULTIPLE, place your chosen objects in the table
multiple_object: that is, place the number of objects in multiple_object->0 and the objects themselves in -->1, . . .
The value GPR_REPARSE should only be returned if you have actually
altered the text you were supposed to be parsing. This is a feature used
internally by the parser when it asks ‚Äė‚ÄėWhich do you mean . . .?‚Äô‚Äô questions, and
you can use it too, but be wary of loops in which the parser eternally changes
and reparses the same text.
¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

4 To parse a token, the parser uses a routine called ParseToken. This behaves
almost exactly like a general parsing routine, and returns the same range of values. For
instance,
ParseToken(ELEMENTARY_TT, NUMBER_TOKEN)

Ôú≤Ôú≤Ôú∑

¬ßÔú≥ÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚĮÔĚęÔĚ•ÔĚģÔĚ≥ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚßÔĚ≤ÔĚ°ÔĚ≠ÔĚ≠ÔĚ°ÔĚ≤
parses exactly as number does: similarly for NOUN_TOKEN, HELD_TOKEN, MULTI_TOKEN,
MULTIHELD_TOKEN, MULTIEXCEPT_TOKEN, MULTIINSIDE_TOKEN and CREATURE_TOKEN.
The call
ParseToken(SCOPE_TT, MyRoutine)
does what scope=MyRoutine does. In fact ParseToken can parse any kind of token,
but these are the only cases which are both useful enough to mention and safe enough
to use. It means you can conveniently write a token which matches, say, either the word
‚Äė‚Äėkit‚Äô‚Äô or any named set of items in scope:
[ KitOrStuff; if (NextWord() == ‚Äôkit‚Äô) return GPR_PREPOSITION;
wn--; return ParseToken(ELEMENTARY_TT, MULTI_TOKEN);
];

‚ÄĘ REFERENCES

Once upon a time, Andrew Clover wrote a neat library extension called "timewait.h"
for parsing times of day, and allowing commands such as ‚Äė‚Äėwait until quarter to three‚Äô‚Äô.
L. Ross Raszewski, Nicholas Daley and Kevin Forchione each tinkered with and
modernised this, so that there are now also "waittime.h" and "timesys.h". Each has
its merits.

Ôú≤Ôú≥Ôúį

¬ß32

Scope and what you can see
He cannot see beyond his own nose. Even the fingers he outstretches from it to the world are (as I shall suggest) often invisible
to him.
‚ÄĒ Max Beerbohm (1872‚Äď1956), of George Bernard Shaw

Time to say what ‚Äė‚Äėin scope‚Äô‚Äô means. This definition is one of the
most important rules of play, because it decides what the player
is allowed to refer to. You can investigate this experimentally by
compiling any game with the debugging suite of verbs included (see
¬ß7) and typing ‚Äė‚Äėscope‚Äô‚Äô in interesting places. ‚Äė‚ÄėIn scope‚Äô‚Äô roughly means ‚Äė‚Äėthe
compass directions, what you‚Äôre carrying and what you can see‚Äô‚Äô. It exactly
means this:
(1) the compass directions;
(2) the player‚Äôs immediate possessions;
(3) if there is light, then the contents of the player‚Äôs visibility ceiling (see ¬ß21 for
definition, but roughly speaking the outermost object containing the player which
remains visible, which is usually the player‚Äôs location);
(4) if there is darkness, then the contents of the library‚Äôs object thedark (by default
there are no such contents, but some designers have been known to move objects
into thedark: see ‚ÄėRuins‚Äô);
(5) if the player is inside a container, then that container;
(6) if O is in scope and is see-through (see ¬ß21), then the contents of O;
(7) if O is in scope, then any object which it ‚Äė‚Äėadds to scope‚Äô‚Äô (see below).

with the proviso that the InScope entry point (see below) can add to or replace
these rules, if you write one.
It‚Äôs significant that rule (3) doesn‚Äôt just say ‚Äė‚Äėwhatever is in the current
location‚Äô‚Äô. For instance, if the player is in a closed cupboard in the Stores
Room, then rule (3) means that the contents of the cupboard are in scope, but
other items in the Stores Room are not.
Even in darkness the player‚Äôs possessions are in scope, so the player can
still turn on a lamp being carried. On the other hand, a player who puts the
lamp on the ground and turns it off then loses the ability to turn it back on
again, because it is out of scope. This can be changed; see below.
4

Compass directions make sense as things as well as directions, and they respond
to names like ‚Äė‚Äėthe south wall‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėthe floor‚Äô‚Äô as well as ‚Äė‚Äėsouth‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėdown‚Äô‚Äô.

Ôú≤Ôú≥ÔúĪ

¬ßÔú≥Ôú≤ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚĮÔĚįÔĚ• ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚ∑ÔĚ®ÔĚ°ÔĚī ÔĚĻÔĚĮÔĚĶ ÔĚ£ÔĚ°ÔĚģ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ•
4 The concealed attribute only hides objects from room descriptions, and doesn‚Äôt
remove them from scope. If you want things to be unreferrable-to, put them somewhere
else!
4

The small print: 1. For ‚Äė‚Äėplayer‚Äô‚Äô, read ‚Äė‚Äėactor‚Äô‚Äô. Thus ‚Äė‚Äėdwarf, drop sword‚Äô‚Äô will
be accepted if the dwarf can see the sword even if the player can‚Äôt. 2. Scope varies
depending on the token being parsed: for the multi- tokens, compass directions are
not in scope; for multiexcept the other object isn‚Äôt in scope; for multiinside only
the contents of the other object are in scope.

¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

Two library routines enable you to see what‚Äôs in scope and what isn‚Äôt. The first,
TestScope(obj, actor), simply returns true or false according to whether
or not obj is in scope. The second is LoopOverScope(routine, actor) and
calls the given routine for each object in scope. In each case the actor given
is optional, and if it‚Äôs omitted, scope is worked out for the player as usual.
‚ÄĘ EXERCISE 97

Implement the debugging suite‚Äôs ‚Äė‚Äėscope‚Äô‚Äô verb, which lists all the objects currently in
scope.

‚ÄĘ EXERCISE 98

Write a ‚Äė‚Äėmegalook‚Äô‚Äô verb, which looks around and examines everything in scope except
the walls, floor and ceiling.

¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

Formally, scope determines what you can talk about, which usually means
what you can see or hear. But what can you touch? Suppose a locked chest
is inside a sealed glass cabinet. The Inform parser will allow the command
‚Äė‚Äėunlock chest with key‚Äô‚Äô and generate the appropriate action, <Unlock chest
key>, because the chest is in scope, so the command at least makes sense.
But it‚Äôs impossible to carry out, because the player can‚Äôt reach through
the solid glass. So the library‚Äôs routine for handling the Unlock action needs to
enforce this. The library does this using a stricter rule called ‚Äė‚Äėtouchability‚Äô‚Äô.
The rule is that you can touch anything in scope unless there‚Äôs a closed
container between you and it. This applies either if you‚Äôre in the container,
or if it is.
Some purely visual actions, such as Examine or LookUnder, don‚Äôt require
touchability. But most actions are tactile, and so are many actions created by
designers. If you want to make your own action routines enforce touchability,
you can call the library routine ObjectIsUntouchable(obj). This either
returns false and prints nothing if there‚Äôs no problem in touching obj, or
Ôú≤Ôú≥Ôú≤

¬ßÔú≥Ôú≤ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚĮÔĚįÔĚ• ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚ∑ÔĚ®ÔĚ°ÔĚī ÔĚĻÔĚĮÔĚĶ ÔĚ£ÔĚ°ÔĚģ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ•
returns true and prints a suitable message, such as ‚Äė‚ÄėThe solid glass cabinet is
in the way.‚Äô‚Äô Thus, the first line of many of the library‚Äôs action routines is:
if (ObjectIsUntouchable(noun)) return;

You can also call ObjectIsUntouchable(obj, true) to simply return true
or false, printing nothing, if you‚Äôd rather provide your own failure message.
¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

The rest of this section is about how to change the scope rules. As usual with
Inform, you can change them globally, but it‚Äôs more efficient and safer to work
locally. To take a typical example: how do we allow the player to ask questions
like the traditional ‚Äė‚Äėwhat is a grue‚Äô‚Äô? The ‚Äė‚Äėgrue‚Äô‚Äô part ought to be parsed as
if it were a noun, so that we could distinguish between, say, a ‚Äė‚Äėgarden grue‚Äô‚Äô
and a ‚Äė‚Äėwild grue‚Äô‚Äô. So it isn‚Äôt good enough to look only at a single word. Here
is one solution:
[ QuerySub; noun.description(); return;
];
[ QueryTopic;
switch (scope_stage) {
1: rfalse;
2: ScopeWithin(questions); rtrue;
3: "At the moment, even the simplest questions confuse you.";
}
];
Object questions;

where the actual questions at any time are the current children of the questions
object, like so:
Object -> "long count"
with name ‚Äôlong‚Äô ‚Äôcount‚Äô,
description "The Long Count is the great Mayan cycle of
time, which began in 3114 BC and will finish with
the world‚Äôs end in 2012 AD.";

(which might be helpful in ‚ÄėRuins‚Äô) and we also have a grammar line:
Verb ‚Äôwhat‚Äô * ‚Äôis‚Äô/‚Äôwas‚Äô scope=QueryTopic -> Query;

The individual questions have short names so that the parser might be able to
say ‚Äė‚ÄėWhich do you mean, the long count or the short count?‚Äô‚Äô if the player
asked ‚Äė‚Äėwhat is the count‚Äô‚Äô. (As it stands this won‚Äôt recognise ‚Äė‚Äėwhat is the
count?‚Äô‚Äô. Conventionally players are supposed not to type question marks or
quotes in their commands. To allow this one could always add ‚Äôcount?‚Äô as
one of the names above.)
Ôú≤Ôú≥Ôú≥

¬ßÔú≥Ôú≤ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚĮÔĚįÔĚ• ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚ∑ÔĚ®ÔĚ°ÔĚī ÔĚĻÔĚĮÔĚĶ ÔĚ£ÔĚ°ÔĚģ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ•
¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

Here is the specification. When the parser reaches scope=Whatever , it calls
the Whatever routine with the variable scope_stage set to 1. The routine
should return true if it is prepared to allow multiple objects to be accepted
here, and false otherwise. (In the example, as we don‚Äôt want ‚Äė‚Äėwhat is
everything‚Äô‚Äô to list all the questions and answers in the game, we return false.)
A little later the parser calls Whatever again with scope_stage now set to
2. Whatever is then obliged to tell the parser which objects are to be in scope.
It can call two parser routines to do this:
ScopeWithin(obj)

which puts everything inside obj, but not obj itself, into scope, and then works
through rules (6) and (7) above, so that it may continue to add the contents of
the contents, and so on; and
PlaceInScope(obj)

which puts just obj into scope. It is perfectly legal to declare something in
scope that ‚Äė‚Äėwould have been in scope anyway‚Äô‚Äô: or something which is in a
different room altogether from the actor concerned, say at the other end of a
telephone line. The scope routine Whatever should then return false if the
nominated items are additional to the usual scope, or true if they are the only
items in scope. (In the example, QueryTopic returns true because it wants
the only items in scope to be its question topics, not the usual miscellany near
the player.)
This is fine if the token is correctly parsed. If not, the parser may choose
to ask the token routine to print a suitable error message, by calling Whatever
with scope_stage set to 3. (In the example, this will happen if the player types
‚Äė‚Äėwhat is the lgon count‚Äô‚Äô, and QueryTopic replies ‚Äė‚ÄėAt the moment, even the
simplest questions confuse you.‚Äô‚Äô, because it comes from a faintly dream-like
game called ‚ÄėBalances‚Äô.)
‚ÄĘ EXERCISE 99

Write a token which puts everything in scope, so that you could have a debugging
‚Äė‚Äėpurloin‚Äô‚Äô which could take anything, regardless of where it was and the rules applying
to it.

¬ßÔú≥Ôú≤ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚĮÔĚįÔĚ• ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚ∑ÔĚ®ÔĚ°ÔĚī ÔĚĻÔĚĮÔĚĶ ÔĚ£ÔĚ°ÔĚģ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ•
4 The global scope rules can be tampered with by providing an entry point routine
called InScope(actor), where actor is the actor whose scope is worked out. In effect,
this defines the ‚Äė‚Äėordinary‚Äô‚Äô scope by making calls to ScopeWithin and PlaceInScope,
then returning true or false, exactly as if it were a scope token at stage 2. For instance,
here as promised is how to change the rule that ‚Äė‚Äėthings you‚Äôve just dropped disappear
in the dark‚Äô‚Äô:
[ InScope person i;
if (person == player && location == thedark)
objectloop (i in parent(player))
if (i has moved) PlaceInScope(i);
rfalse;
];
With this routine added, objects near the player in a dark room are in scope only if
they have moved (that is, have been held by the player in the past), and even then are
in scope only to the player, not to anyone else.

4 The token scope=hRoutinei takes precedence over InScope, which will only be
reached if the routine returns false to signify ‚Äė‚Äėcarry on‚Äô‚Äô.
44 There are seven reasons why InScope might be being called; the scope_reason
variable is set to the current one:
PARSING_REASON

TALKING_REASON
EACHTURN_REASON
REACT_BEFORE_REASON
REACT_AFTER_REASON
TESTSCOPE_REASON
LOOPOVERSCOPE_REASON

the early stages (before the verb has been decided) and later
on the action which would result from a successful match.

(see the end of ¬ß18 for exercises using this).
When running each_turn routines for anything nearby, at
the end of each turn.
When running react_before.
When running react_after.
When performing a TestScope.
When performing a LoopOverScope.

‚ÄĘ 44EXERCISE 101
Code the following puzzle. In an initially dark room there is a light switch. Provided
you‚Äôve seen the switch at some time in the past, you can turn it on and off ‚Äď but before
you‚Äôve ever seen it, you can‚Äôt. Inside the room is nothing you can see, but you can hear
the distinctive sound of a dwarf breathing. If you tell this dwarf to turn the light on, he

Ôú≤Ôú≥ÔúĶ

¬ßÔú≥Ôú≤ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚĮÔĚįÔĚ• ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚ∑ÔĚ®ÔĚ°ÔĚī ÔĚĻÔĚĮÔĚĶ ÔĚ£ÔĚ°ÔĚģ ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ•
¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

Each object has the ability to drag other objects into scope whenever it is
in scope. This is especially useful for giving objects component parts: e.g.,
giving a washing-machine a temperature dial. (The dial can‚Äôt be a child object
because that would throw it in with the clothes: and it ought to be attached
to the machine in case the machine is moved from place to place.) For this
purpose, the property add_to_scope may contain a list of objects to add.
4

Alternatively, it may contain a routine. This routine can then call AddToScope(x)
to add any object x to scope. It may not, however, call ScopeWithin or any other
scoping routines.

44 Scope addition does not occur for an object moved into scope by an explicit call
to PlaceInScope, since this must allow complete freedom in scope selections. But it
does happen when objects are moved in scope by calls to ScopeWithin(domain).

¬ß33

Helping the parser out of trouble

Once you begin programming the parser on a large scale, you soon
reach the point where the parser‚Äôs ordinary error messages no longer
appear sensible. The ParserError entry point can change the rules
even at this last hurdle: it takes one argument, the error type, and
should return true to tell the parser to shut up, because a better error message
has already been printed, or false, to tell the parser to print its usual message.
The error types are defined as constants:
STUCK_PE
UPTO_PE
NUMBER_PE
CANTSEE_PE
TOOLIT_PE
NOTHELD_PE
MULTI_PE
MMULTI_PE
VAGUE_PE
EXCEPT_PE
ANIMA_PE
VERB_PE
SCENERY_PE
ITGONE_PE
JUNKAFTER_PE
TOOFEW_PE
NOTHING_PE
ASKSCOPE_PE

I didn‚Äôt understand that sentence.
I only understood you as far as. . .
I didn‚Äôt understand that number.
You can‚Äôt see any such thing.
You seem to have said too little!
You aren‚Äôt holding that!
You can‚Äôt use multiple objects with that verb.
You can only use multiple objects once on a line.
I‚Äôm not sure what ‚Äėit‚Äô refers to.
You excepted something not included anyway!
You can only do that to something animate.
That‚Äôs not a verb I recognise.
That‚Äôs not something you need to refer to. . .
You can‚Äôt see ‚Äėit‚Äô (the whatever) at the moment.
I didn‚Äôt understand the way that finished.
Only five of those are available.
Nothing to do!
whatever the scope routine prints

Each unsuccessful grammar line ends in one of these conditions. By the time
the parser wants to print an error, every one of the grammar lines in a verb will
have failed. The error message chosen it prints is the most ‚Äė‚Äėinteresting‚Äô‚Äô one:
meaning, lowest down this list.
If a general parsing routine you have written returns GPR_FAIL, then
the grammar line containing it normally ends in plain STUCK_PE, the least
interesting of all errors (unless you did something like calling the library‚Äôs
ParseToken routine before giving up, which might have set a more interesting
error like CANTSEE_PE). But you can choose to create a new error and put it in
the parser‚Äôs variable etype, as in the following example:
[ Degrees d;
d = TryNumber(wn++);

Ôú≤Ôú≥Ôú∑

¬ßÔú≥Ôú≥ ÔĚ®ÔĚ•ÔĚ¨ÔĚįÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚß ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ ÔĚĮÔĚĶÔĚī ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚīÔĚ≤ÔĚĮÔĚĶÔĚĘÔĚ¨ÔĚ•
if (d == -1000) return GPR_FAIL;
if (d <= 360) { parsed_number = d; return GPR_NUMBER; }
etype = "There are only 360 degrees in a circle.";
return GPR_FAIL;
];

This parses a number of degrees between 0 and 360. Although etype normally
only holds values like VERB_PE, which are numbers lower than 100, here we‚Äôve
set it equal to a string. As this will be a value that the parser doesn‚Äôt recognise,
we need to write a ParserError routine that will take care of it, by reacting to
a string in the obvious way ‚Äď printing it out.
[ ParserError error_type;
if (error_type ofclass String) print_ret (string) error_type;
rfalse;
];

This will result in conversation like so:
>steer down
I didn‚Äôt understand that sentence.
>steer 385
There are only 360 degrees in a circle.

In the first case, Degrees failed without setting any special error message on
finding that the second word wasn‚Äôt a number; in the second case it gave the
new, specific error message.
¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

The VAGUE_PE and ITGONE_PE errors apply to all pronouns (in English, ‚Äė‚Äėit‚Äô‚Äô,
‚Äė‚Äėhim‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėher‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėthem‚Äô‚Äô). The variable vague_word contains the dictionary
address of whichever pronoun is involved (‚Äôit‚Äô, ‚Äôhim‚Äô and so on).
You can find out the current setting of a pronoun using the library‚Äôs
PronounValue routine: for instance, PronounValue(‚Äôit‚Äô) gives the object
which ‚Äė‚Äėit‚Äô‚Äô currently refers to, possibly nothing. Similarly SetPronoun(‚Äôit‚Äô,
magic_ruby) would set ‚Äė‚Äėit‚Äô‚Äô to mean the magic ruby object. You might want
this because, when something like a magic ruby suddenly appears in the
middle of a turn, players will habitually call it ‚Äė‚Äėit‚Äô‚Äô. A better way to adjust
the pronouns is to call PronounNotice(magic_ruby), which sets whatever
pronouns are appropriate. That is, it works out if the object is a thing or
a person, of what number and gender, which pronouns apply to it in the
parser‚Äôs current language, and so on. In code predating Inform 6.1 you may
see variables called itobj, himobj and herobj holding the English pronoun
values: these still work properly, but please use the modern system in new
games.
Ôú≤Ôú≥Ôúł

¬ßÔú≥Ôú≥ ÔĚ®ÔĚ•ÔĚ¨ÔĚįÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚß ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ ÔĚĮÔĚĶÔĚī ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚīÔĚ≤ÔĚĮÔĚĶÔĚĘÔĚ¨ÔĚ•
¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

4

The Inform parser resolves ambiguous object names with a pragmatic algorithm
which has evolved over the years (see below). Experience also shows that no two
people ever quite agree on what the parser should ‚Äė‚Äėnaturally‚Äô‚Äô do. Designers have an
opportunity to influence this by providing an entry point routine called ChooseObjects:
ChooseObjects(obj, code)

is called in two circumstances. If code is false or true, the parser is considering
including the given obj in an ‚Äė‚Äėall‚Äô‚Äô: false means the parser has decided against, true
means it has decided in favour. The routine should reply
0
1
2

to accept the parser‚Äôs decision;
to force the object to be included; or
to force the object to be excluded.

It may want to decide using verb_word (the variable storing the current verb word, e.g.,
‚Äôtake‚Äô) and action_to_be, which is the action which would happen if the current line
of grammar were successfully matched.
The other circumstance is when code is 2. This means the parser is choosing
between a list of items which made equally good matches against some text, and would
like a hint. ChooseObjects should then return a number from 0 to 9 to give obj a score
for how appropriate it is.
For instance, some designers would prefer ‚Äė‚Äėtake all‚Äô‚Äô not to attempt to take
scenery objects (which Inform, and the parsers in most of the Infocom games, will
do). Let us code this, and also teach the parser that edible things are more likely to be
eaten than inedible ones:
[ ChooseObjects obj code;
if (code < 2) { if (obj has scenery) return 2; rfalse; }
if (action_to_be == ##Eat && obj has edible) return 3;
if (obj hasnt scenery) return 2;
return 1;
];
Scenery is now excluded from ‚Äė‚Äėall‚Äô‚Äô lists; and is further penalised in that non-scenery
objects are always preferred over scenery, all else being equal. Most objects score 2 but
edible things in the context of eating score 3, so ‚Äė‚Äėeat black‚Äô‚Äô will now always choose a
Black Forest gateau in preference to a black rod with a rusty iron star on the end.
‚ÄĘ 4EXERCISE 105
Allow ‚Äė‚Äėlock‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėunlock‚Äô‚Äô to infer their second objects without being told, if there‚Äôs
an obvious choice (because the player‚Äôs only carrying one key), but to issue a disambiguation question otherwise. (Use Extend, not ChooseObjects.)

‚ÄĘ 4EXERCISE 106
Joyce Haslam‚Äôs Inform edition of the classic Acornsoft game ‚ÄėGateway to Karos‚Äô
requires a class called FaintlyLitRoom for rooms so dimly illuminated that ‚Äė‚Äėtake all‚Äô‚Äô
is impossible. How might this work?

¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

44 Suppose we have a set of objects which have all matched equally well against the
textual input, so that some knowledge of the game world is needed to resolve which
of the objects ‚Äď possibly only one, possibly more ‚Äď is or are intended. Deciding this is
called ‚Äė‚Äėdisambiguation‚Äô‚Äô, and here in full are the rules used by library 6/10 to do it.
The reader is cautioned that after six years, these rules are still evolving.
(1) Call an object ‚Äė‚Äėgood‚Äô‚Äô according to a rule depending on what kind of token is
being matched:
held
Good if its parent is the actor.
multiheld
Good if its parent is the actor.
multiexcept Good if not also the second object, if that‚Äôs known yet.
multiinside Good if not inside the second object, if that‚Äôs known yet.
creature
Good if animate, or if the proposed action is Ask,
Answer, Tell or AskFor and the object is talkable.
other tokens
All objects are good.
If only a single object is good, this is immediately chosen.
(2) If the token is creature and no objects are good, fail the token altogether, as no
choice can make sense.
(3) Objects which don‚Äôt fit ‚Äė‚Äėdescriptors‚Äô‚Äô used by the player are removed:
if ‚Äė‚Äėmy‚Äô‚Äô, an object whose parent isn‚Äôt the actor is discarded;
if ‚Äė‚Äėthat‚Äô‚Äô, an object whose parent isn‚Äôt the actor‚Äôs location is discarded;
if ‚Äė‚Äėlit‚Äô‚Äô, an object which hasn‚Äôt light is discarded;
if ‚Äė‚Äėunlit‚Äô‚Äô, an object which has light is discarded;
if ‚Äė‚Äėhis‚Äô‚Äô or some similar possessive pronoun, an object not owned by the
person implied is discarded.
Thus ‚Äė‚Äėhis lit torches‚Äô‚Äô will invoke two of these rules at once.
(4) If there are no objects left, fail the token, as no choice can make sense.
(5) It is now certain that the token will not fail. The remaining objects are assigned a
score as follows:
(i) 1000 √ó C points, where C is the return value of ChooseObjects(object,2).
(0 ‚Č§ C ‚Č§ 9. If the designer doesn‚Äôt provide this entry point at all then
C = 0.)
(ii) 500 points for being ‚Äė‚Äėgood‚Äô‚Äô (see (1) above).
(iii) 100 points for not having concealed.

Ôú≤ÔúīÔúį

¬ßÔú≥Ôú≥ ÔĚ®ÔĚ•ÔĚ¨ÔĚįÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚß ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ ÔĚĮÔĚĶÔĚī ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚīÔĚ≤ÔĚĮÔĚĶÔĚĘÔĚ¨ÔĚ•
(iv) P points depending on the object‚Äôs position:

Ô£Ī
A
Ô£ī
Ô£≤

if object belongs to the actor,
L if object belongs to the actor‚Äôs visibility ceiling,
P =
Ô£ī
Ô£≥ 20 if object belongs anywhere else except the compass,
0
if object belongs to the compass.

(Recall that ‚Äė‚Äėvisibility ceiling‚Äô‚Äô usually means ‚Äė‚Äėlocation‚Äô‚Äô and that the objects
belonging to the compass are exactly the compass directions.) The values A
and L depend on the token being parsed:

n

A = 60 L = 40
A = 40 L = 60

for held or multiheld tokens,
otherwise.

(v) 10 points for not having scenery.
(vi) 5 points for not being the actor object.
(vii) 1 point if the object‚Äôs gender, number and animation (GNA) matches one
possibility implied by some pronoun typed by the player: for instance ‚Äė‚Äėthem‚Äô‚Äô
in English implying plural, or ‚Äė‚Äėle‚Äô‚Äô in French implying masculine singular.
(6d) In ‚Äė‚Äėdefinite mode‚Äô‚Äô, such as if the player has typed a definite article like ‚Äė‚Äėthe‚Äô‚Äô, if
any single object has highest score, choose that object.
(7ip) The following rule applies only in indefinite mode and provided the player has
typed something definitely implying a plural, such as the words ‚Äė‚Äėall‚Äô‚Äô or ‚Äė‚Äėthree‚Äô‚Äô
or ‚Äė‚Äėcoins‚Äô‚Äô. Here the parser already has a target number of objects to choose:
for instance 3 for ‚Äė‚Äėthree‚Äô‚Äô, or the special value of 100, meaning ‚Äė‚Äėan unlimited
number‚Äô‚Äô, for ‚Äė‚Äėall‚Äô‚Äô or ‚Äė‚Äėcoins‚Äô‚Äô.
Go through the list of objects in ‚Äė‚Äėbest guess‚Äô‚Äô order (see below). Mark each as
‚Äė‚Äėaccept‚Äô‚Äô unless:
(i) it has worn or concealed;
(ii) or the action is Take or Remove and the object is held by the actor;
(iii) or the token is multiheld or multiexcept and the object isn‚Äôt held by
the actor;
(iv) or the target number is ‚Äė‚Äėunlimited‚Äô‚Äô and S/20 (rounded down to the nearest
integer) has fallen below its maximum value, where S is the score of the
object.
The entry point ChooseObjects(object,accept_flag) is now called and can
overrule the ‚Äė‚Äėaccept‚Äô‚Äô/‚Äė‚Äėreject‚Äô‚Äô decision either way. We keep accepting objects like
this until the target is reached, or proves impossible to reach.
(8) The objects are now grouped so that any set of indistinguishable objects forms a
single group. ‚Äė‚ÄėIndistinguishable‚Äô‚Äô means that no further text typed by the player
could clarify which is meant (see ¬ß29). Note that there is no reason to suppose
that two indistinguishable objects have the same score, because they might be in
different places.

Ôú≤ÔúīÔúĪ

¬ßÔú≥Ôú≥ ÔĚ®ÔĚ•ÔĚ¨ÔĚįÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚß ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤ ÔĚĮÔĚĶÔĚī ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚīÔĚ≤ÔĚĮÔĚĶÔĚĘÔĚ¨ÔĚ•
(9d) In definite mode, we know that there‚Äôs a tie for highest score, as otherwise a choice
would have been made at step (6d). If these highest-scoring objects belong to
more than one group, then ask the player to choose which group:
You can see a bronze coin and four gold coins here.
>get coin
Which do you mean, the bronze coin or a gold coin?
>gold
The player‚Äôs response is inserted textually into the original input and the parsing
begins again from scratch with ‚Äė‚Äėget gold coin‚Äô‚Äô instead of ‚Äė‚Äėget coin‚Äô‚Äô.
(10) Only two possibilities remain: either (i) we are in indefinite but singular mode,
or (ii) we are in definite mode and there is a tie for highest-scoring object and
all of these equal-highest objects belong to the same group. Either way, choose
the ‚Äė‚Äėbest guess‚Äô‚Äô object (see below). Should this parsing attempt eventually prove
successful, print up an ‚Äė‚Äėinference‚Äô‚Äô on screen, such as
>get key
(the copper key)
only if the number of groups found in (8) is more than 1.
(BG) It remains to define ‚Äė‚Äėbest guess‚Äô‚Äô. From a set of objects, the best guess is the
highest-scoring one not yet guessed; and if several objects have equal highest
scores, it is the earliest one to have been matched by the parser. In practice this
means the one most recently taken or dropped, because the parser tries to match
against objects by traversing the object-tree, and the most recently moved object
tends to be the first in the list of children of its parent.

‚ÄĘ REFERENCES

See ‚ÄėBalances‚Äô for a usage of ParserError.
‚ÄĘInfocom‚Äôs parser typically produces
error messages like ‚Äė‚ÄėI don‚Äôt know the word ‚Äėtarantula‚Äô.‚Äô‚Äô when the player types a word
not in the game‚Äôs dictionary, and some designers prefer this style to Inform‚Äôs givenothing-away approach (Inform tries not to let the player carry out experiments to see
what is, and what is not, in the dictionary). Neil Cerutti‚Äôs "dunno.h" library extension
restores the Infocom format. ‚ÄĘThe library extension "calyx_adjectives.h", which
resolves ambiguities in parsing by placing more weight on matches with nouns than
with adjectives, works by using ChooseObjects.

‚ÄĒ ‚ÄėZork I: Das Gro√üe Unterweltreich‚Äô

¬ß34

Linguistics and the Inform parser

¬°Bienvenido a Aventura! Despite the English-language bias of early
computers and their manuals, interactive fiction has a culture and
a history beyond English, not least in Germany. Like the Monty
Python team and the Beatles, Infocom made a German translation
of their defining work, when in early 1988 Jeff O‚ÄôNeill coded up ‚ÄėZork I:
Das Gro√üe Unterweltreich‚Äô. It came at a sorry time in Infocom‚Äôs fortunes
and remains officially unreleased, in part because the translator recruited had
rendered the text in a stilted, business-German manner, though a beta-test
of the story file circulates to this day. But O‚ÄôNeill‚Äôs work was not in vain,
as it left another important legacy: an upgrading of the Z-machine format
to allow for accented characters, which opened the door to non-English IF
on the Z-machine. Jose Luiz Diaz‚Äôs translation of ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô into Spanish,
as ‚ÄėAventura‚Äô, stimulated much of the 1996 development of Inform as a
multilingual system, and Toni Arnold‚Äôs game ‚ÄėRummelplatzgeschichte‚Äô (1998)
also deserves mention, as does advice from Inform users across four continents,
among them TorbjoŐąrn Andersson, Joachim Baumann, Paul David Doherty,
Bjorn Gustavsson, Aapo Haapanen, Ralf Herrmann, J. P. Ikaheimonen, Ilario
Nardinocchi, Bob Newell, Giovanni Riccardi and Linards Ticmanis. If nothing
else, I am glad to have learned the palindromic Finnish word for soap dealer,
‚Äė‚Äėsaippuakauppias‚Äô‚Äô.
The standard English-language release of the Inform library now consists
of eight files of code. Of these eight, only two need to be replaced to make
a translation to another language: "Grammar.h", which contains grammars
for English verbs like ‚Äė‚Äėtake‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėdrop‚Äô‚Äô; and a ‚Äė‚Äėlanguage definition file‚Äô‚Äô
called "English.h". For instance, in Ilario Nardinocchi‚Äôs translation these
Ôú≤ÔúīÔú≥

¬ßÔú≥Ôúī ÔĚ¨ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚßÔĚĶÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚ£ÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ¶ÔĚĮÔĚ≤ÔĚ≠ ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤
two files are replaced by "ItalianG.h" and "Italian.h", in Jose Luis Diaz‚Äôs
translation they become "SpanishG.h" and "Spanish.h" and so on. Language
definition files can be useful for more, or rather less, than just translation. ‚ÄėThe
Tempest‚Äô (1997), for instance, uses a language definition file to enable it to
speak in Early Modern English verse and to recognise pronouns like ‚Äė‚Äėthee‚Äô‚Äô
and ‚Äė‚Äėthy‚Äô‚Äô. A suitable language definition file could also change the persona
of an Inform game from second-person (‚Äė‚ÄėYou fall into a pit!‚Äô‚Äô) to first-person
(‚Äė‚ÄėI have fallen into a pit!‚Äô‚Äô) or third (‚Äė‚ÄėBilbo falls into a pit!‚Äô‚Äô), or from present
to past tenses, as Jamie Murphy‚Äôs game ‚ÄėLeopold the Minstrel‚Äô (1996) did.
This section goes into the linguistics of the Inform parser, and how to add
new grammatical concepts to it using grammar tokens. The next goes into
full-scale translation and how to write new language definition files.
¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

Language is complex, computers are simple. Modern English is a mostly
non-inflected language, meaning that words tend not to alter their spelling
according to their usage, but even here the parser has to go to some trouble to
cope with one of its remaining inflections (‚Äė‚Äėtake coin‚Äô‚Äô but ‚Äė‚Äėtake six coins‚Äô‚Äô:
see ¬ß29). The range of variation in human languages is large and as many are
heavily inflected the task at first seems hopeless.‚Ä†
On the other hand, Inform is mainly used with Romance-family languages,
where commands are formed roughly as they are in English. The language
understood by the parser is a simple one, called Informese. It has three
genders, two numbers, a concept of animate versus inanimate nouns and a
clear understanding of articles and pronouns, but all verbs are imperative,
the only tense is the present, there are no cases of nouns (but see ¬ß35) and
adjectives are not distinguished from nouns (but see ¬ß26). Informese is based
on a small part of English, but the proposition of this chapter is that (with
some effort) you can find Informese at the core of many other languages as
well.
Changes of vocabulary are obviously needed: for instance, where an
English game recognises ‚Äė‚Äėother‚Äô‚Äô, a French one must recognise ‚Äė‚Äėautre‚Äô‚Äô. But,
as the following example shows, vocabulary changes are not enough:
jetez la boule dedans

throw the ball into it (French)

has no word-for-word translation into Informese, because ‚Äė‚Äėdedans‚Äô‚Äô (into it)
is a pronominal adverb, and Informese doesn‚Äôt have pronominal adverbs.
‚Ä† In fact the difficult languages to parse are not those with subtleties of spelling but
those where even word-recognition can be a matter of context and guesswork, such as
Hebrew, where all vowels are conventionally omitted.

Ôú≤ÔúīÔúī

¬ßÔú≥Ôúī ÔĚ¨ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚßÔĚĶÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚ£ÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ¶ÔĚĮÔĚ≤ÔĚ≠ ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤
Instead, a transformational rule like this one must be applied:
dedans

inside it

7‚Üí

dans lui

Transformational rules like this one bring new grammatical structures into the
Inform parser. The rest of this section is occupied with describing what is
present already.
¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

The following is a short grammar of Informese. Both here and in the General Index,
grammatical concepts understood by the parser are written in angle brackets hlike soi.
(1) Commands
A command to an Inform game should be one of:
hoops-wordi hwordi
haction phrasei
hnoun phrasei, haction phrasei
An hoops-wordi corrects the last command by putting the hwordi in to replace whatever
seemed to be incorrect. In "English.h", the only words in the hoops-wordi category
are ‚Äė‚Äėoops‚Äô‚Äô and its abbreviation ‚Äė‚Äėo‚Äô‚Äô. An haction phrasei instructs the player to perform
an action, unless it is preceded by a hnoun phrasei and a comma, in which case someone
else is instructed to perform an action.
An haction phrasei consists of a sequence of verb phrases, divided up by full stops
or then-words: a hthen-wordi is a word like the English ‚Äė‚Äėthen‚Äô‚Äô or a full stop. For
instance ‚Äė‚Äėtake sword. east. put sword in stone‚Äô‚Äô is broken into a sequence of three verb
phrases, each parsed and acted on in turn. (It‚Äôs important not to parse them all at once:
the meaning of the noun phrase ‚Äė‚Äėstone‚Äô‚Äô depends on where the player is by then.)
(2) Verb phrases
A hverb phrasei is one of:
hagain-wordi
himperative verbi hgrammar linei
Again-words are another category: in "English.h" these are ‚Äė‚Äėagain‚Äô‚Äô and its abbreviation ‚Äė‚Äėg‚Äô‚Äô. An hagain-wordi is understood as ‚Äė‚Äėthe hverb phrasei most recently typed in
which wasn‚Äôt an hagain-wordi‚Äô‚Äô.
The imperative is the form of the verb used for orders or instructions. In English
the imperative (‚Äė‚Äėopen the window‚Äô‚Äô) looks the same as the infinitive (‚Äė‚Äėto open‚Äô‚Äô), but
in most languages they differ (French ‚Äė‚Äėouvrez‚Äô‚Äô is imperative, ‚Äė‚Äėouvrir‚Äô‚Äô infinitive). Even
in many languages where verbs usually follow objects, such as Latin, the imperative
comes at the start of a verb phrase, and Informese insists on this. Informese also

Ôú≤ÔúīÔúĶ

¬ßÔú≥Ôúī ÔĚ¨ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚßÔĚĶÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚ£ÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ¶ÔĚĮÔĚ≤ÔĚ≠ ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤
wants the himperative verbi to be a single word, but programming can get around both
requirements.
Grammar lines are sequences of tokens. Each token results in one of four
grammatically different outcomes:
hnoun phrasei
hprepositioni
hnumberi
hunparsed texti
For instance, a successful match for the tokens noun or multiheld would produce
a hnoun phrasei, whereas a match for ‚Äôinto‚Äô would produce a hprepositioni. Note
that a general parsing routine can produce any of these four outcomes.
(3) Prepositions
Any word written in quotes as a grammar token. This is normally also a preposition in
the ordinary grammatical sense, but not always, as the ‚Äė‚Äėpress charges‚Äô‚Äô example in ¬ß30
shows. In "English.h", ‚Äė‚Äėlook under table‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėswitch on radio‚Äô‚Äô contain two words
considered to be prepositions in Informese: ‚Äė‚Äėunder‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėon‚Äô‚Äô.
(4) Numbers
Include at least the numbers 1 to 20 written out in words. ‚Äė‚ÄėAt least‚Äô‚Äô because a language
definition file is free to include more, but should not include less.
(5) Noun phrases
A string of words which refer to a single object or collection of objects, with more or
less exactness. Here are some typical examples of "English.h" noun phrases:
it
rucksack
brown bag, pepper
a box and the other compass
nine silver coins
everything except the rucksack
smooth stones
A noun phrase is a list of basic noun phrases:
hbasic npi hconnectivei hbasic npi hconnectivei . . . hconnectivei hbasic npi
and there are two kinds of connective: an hand-wordi (conjunction), and a hbut-wordi
(disjunction). The Inform parser always regards a comma in a hnoun phrasei (other
than one used at the start of a command: see (1) above) as an hand-wordi, and the
definition of "English.h" gives ‚Äė‚Äėand‚Äô‚Äô as another. "English.h" has two hbut-wordsi:
‚Äė‚Äėbut‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėexcept‚Äô‚Äô.

Ôú≤ÔúīÔú∂

¬ßÔú≥Ôúī ÔĚ¨ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚßÔĚĶÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚ£ÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ¶ÔĚĮÔĚ≤ÔĚ≠ ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤
hNoun phrasesi being parsed are assigned several properties. They are declared
definite if they carry no article, or a definite article which is not qualified by an all-word
or a demanding number, and are otherwise indefinite. (Except that a noun-phrase
containing a dictionary word flagged as likely to be referring to plural objects, such
as ‚Äôcrowns//p‚Äô, is always indefinite.) Definiteness affects disambiguation and the
parser‚Äôs willingness to make guesses, as the description of the parser‚Äôs disambiguation
algorithm at the end of ¬ß33 shows.
Indefinite noun phrases also have a target quantity of objects being referred to:
this is normally 1, but 7 for ‚Äė‚Äėseven stones‚Äô‚Äô and 100, used internally to mean ‚Äė‚Äėas
many as possible‚Äô‚Äô, for ‚Äė‚Äėcrowns‚Äô‚Äô or ‚Äė‚Äėall the swords‚Äô‚Äô. Noun phrases also have a
gender-number-animation combination, or ‚Äė‚ÄėGNA‚Äô‚Äô:
Gender: in most European languages, nouns divide up into masculine, feminine
or neuter, the three genders in Informese. Gender is important when parsing noun
phrases because it can distinguish otherwise identical nouns, as in French: ‚Äė‚Äėle faux‚Äô‚Äô,
the forgery, ‚Äė‚Äėla faux‚Äô‚Äô, the scythe. As in German, there may be no satisfactory way to
determine the gender of a noun by any automatic rules: see the next section for how
Inform assigns genders to nouns.
Number: singular (‚Äė‚Äėthe hat‚Äô‚Äô) or plural (‚Äė‚Äėthe grapes‚Äô‚Äô). Individual objects in Inform
games can have names of either number. Languages with more than two numbers are
rare, but Hebrew has a ‚Äė‚Äėpair of‚Äô‚Äô number. This would have to be translated into a
demanding number (see (7d) below) for Informese.
Animation: Informese distinguishes between the animate (people and higher
animals) and the inanimate (objects, plants and lower animals).
With three genders, two numbers and two animations, Informese has twelve
possible GNA combinations, and these are internally represented by the numbers 0 to
11:
0 animate
singular
1
2
3
plural
4
5
6 inanimate singular
7
8
9
plural
10
11

masculine
feminine
neuter
masculine
feminine
neuter
masculine
feminine
neuter
masculine
feminine
neuter

Not all possible GNAs occur in all natural languages. In English, cases 6, 7, 9 and 10
never occur, except perhaps that ships are sometimes called ‚Äė‚Äėshe‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėher‚Äô‚Äô without
being animate (GNA 7). In French, 2, 5, 8 and 11 never occur. The parser actually
works by assigning sets of possible GNA values to each noun phrase: so, in French,

Ôú≤ÔúīÔú∑

¬ßÔú≥Ôúī ÔĚ¨ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚßÔĚĶÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚ£ÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ¶ÔĚĮÔĚ≤ÔĚ≠ ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤
‚Äė‚Äėle faux‚Äô‚Äô carries the set {6}, while the more ambiguous noun phrase ‚Äė‚Äėles‚Äô‚Äô carries
{3, 4, 9, 10}.
(6) Basic noun phrases
These take the following form, in which both lists can have any number of words in,
including none, and in any order:
hlist of descriptorsi hlist of nounsi
For instance ‚Äė‚Äėthe balloon‚Äô‚Äô has one descriptor and one noun; ‚Äė‚Äėred balloon‚Äô‚Äô has just
two nouns; ‚Äė‚Äėall‚Äô‚Äô has just one descriptor.
(7) Descriptors
There are five kinds of hdescriptori, as follows:
(a) An harticlei is a word indicating whether a particular object is being referred to, or
merely one of a range. Thus there are two kinds of article, definite and indefinite.
"English.h" has four articles: ‚Äė‚Äėthe‚Äô‚Äô is definite, while ‚Äė‚Äėa‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėan‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėsome‚Äô‚Äô are
indefinite.
(b) An hall-wordi is a word which behaves like the English word ‚Äė‚Äėall‚Äô‚Äô, that is, which
refers to a whole range of objects. Informese, like some natural languages (such as
Tagalog), handles this as a kind of article but which pluralises what follows it.
(c) An hother-wordi is a word behaving like ‚Äė‚Äėother‚Äô‚Äô, which Informese understands
as ‚Äė‚Äėother than the one I am holding‚Äô‚Äô. Thus, if the player is holding a sword in a
room where there‚Äôs also a sword on the floor, then ‚Äė‚Äėexamine other sword‚Äô‚Äô would
refer to the one on the floor.
(d) A hdemanding numberi is a word like ‚Äė‚Äėnine‚Äô‚Äô in ‚Äė‚Äėnine bronze coins‚Äô‚Äô, which
demands that a certain number of items are needed.
(e) A hpossessive adjectivei is a word indicating ownership by someone or something
whose meaning is held in a pronoun. Among others "English.h" has ‚Äė‚Äėmy‚Äô‚Äô
(belonging to ‚Äė‚Äėme‚Äô‚Äô); French has ‚Äė‚Äėson‚Äô‚Äô (belonging to ‚Äė‚Äėlui‚Äô‚Äô). Informese also
counts hdemonstrative adjectivesi like ‚Äė‚Äėthis‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėthese‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėthat‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėthose‚Äô‚Äô as
possessives, though demonstratives are hardly ever used by players and may not
be worth providing in other languages. In Spanish, for instance, there would have
to be twelve, for ‚Äė‚Äėthis‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėthat‚Äô‚Äô (nearby) and ‚Äė‚Äėthat‚Äô‚Äô (far away), each in masculine,
feminine, singular and plural forms; and the structure of ‚Äė‚Äėcelui-ci‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėcelui-la‚Äô‚Äô
in French is too complex to be worth the effort of parsing.
(8) Nouns
There are three kinds of hnouni, as follows:
(a) A hnamei is a word matched against particular objects. Unless an object has a
parse_name routine attached, which complicates matters, these will be the words
in its name array. For instance:
Object -> "blue box" with name ‚Äôblue‚Äô ‚Äôbox‚Äô;

Ôú≤ÔúīÔúł

¬ßÔú≥Ôúī ÔĚ¨ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚßÔĚĶÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚ£ÔĚ≥ ÔĚ°ÔĚģÔĚ§ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ¶ÔĚĮÔĚ≤ÔĚ≠ ÔĚįÔĚ°ÔĚ≤ÔĚ≥ÔĚ•ÔĚ≤
has two possible names in Informese, ‚Äė‚Äėblue‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėbox‚Äô‚Äô.
(b) A hme-wordi is a word which behaves like the English word ‚Äė‚Äėme‚Äô‚Äô, that is, which
refers to the player-object. Most languages would think these are just examples of
relative pronouns, but Informese considers them to be in a category of their own.
Note that they refer to the player, whoever is addressed: in ‚Äė‚Äėmark, give me the
bomb‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėme‚Äô‚Äô refers to the speaker, not to Mark.
(c) A hpronouni is a word which stands in the place of nouns and can only be
understood with reference back to what has previously been said. ‚Äė‚Äėit‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėhim‚Äô‚Äô,
‚Äė‚Äėher‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėthem‚Äô‚Äô are all pronouns in "English.h". (Though ‚Äė‚Äėher‚Äô‚Äô can also be
a possessive adjective, as in (7e) above.)

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

It is worth mentioning a number of grammatical features which are not contained in
Informese, along with some ways to simulate them.
adverbs such as ‚Äė‚Äėquickly‚Äô‚Äô in ‚Äė‚Äėrun quickly east‚Äô‚Äô. These are not difficult to implement:
Verb ‚Äôrun‚Äô
* noun=ADirection -> Go
* ‚Äôquickly‚Äô noun=ADirection -> GoQuickly
* noun=ADirection ‚Äôquickly‚Äô -> GoQuickly;
However, ‚Äė‚ÄėThe authors of Zork have thought about several possible extensions to the
Zork parser. One that has come up many times is to add adverbs. A player should be
able to do the following:
>go north quietly
You sneak past a sleeping lion who sniffs but doesn‚Äôt wake up.
The problem is to think of reasons why you would not do everything ‚Äėquietly‚Äô,
‚Äėcarefully‚Äô or whatever.‚Äô‚Äô (P. David Lebling, ‚Äė‚ÄėZork and the Future of Computerized
Fantasy Simulations‚Äô‚Äô, Byte, December 1980.) A further problem is the impracticality
of modelling the game world closely enough to differentiate between ways to achieve
the same action. In Melbourne House‚Äôs ‚ÄėThe Hobbit‚Äô adverbs influence the probability
of success in randomised events, so for instance ‚Äė‚Äėthrow rope vigorously across river‚Äô‚Äô
is more likely to succeed than ‚Äė‚Äėthrow rope across river‚Äô‚Äô, but those few players who
discovered this were not pleased. Twenty years on from ‚ÄėZork‚Äô, adverbs remain largely
unused in the medium.
adjectives are not distinguished from nouns, although it can be useful to do so when
resolving ambiguities. See ¬ß28 for remedies.
genitives: objects are not normally named by description of their circumstances, so ‚Äė‚Äėthe
box on the floor‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėthe priest‚Äôs hat‚Äô‚Äô would not normally be understood. Designers
can still define objects like
Object -> "priest‚Äôs hat"
with name ‚Äôhat‚Äô ‚Äôpriest^s‚Äô;
