The design of puzzles
The odyssey of ‚ÄėZork: Mimesis‚Äô begins in a field behind a white
house. You climb in through an open window, take the water and
sack lunch from the table, go in the living room and move the rug
aside to reveal ‚Äď a blank floor! . . . Soon the owner of the house
‚Äď an underemployed, alcoholic bricklayer ‚Äď is covering you with a
shotgun as his unfaithful, neurotic wife dials 911. The puzzle-free,
super-literary action continues as you are funneled through the
criminal justice system. . .
‚ÄĒ Roger Giner-Sorolla, conceding that mimesis is not everything

Without puzzles, or problems, or mechanisms to allow the player
to receive the text a little at a time ‚Äď whichever phrase you prefer
‚Äď there is no interaction. Inevitably, puzzles are obstacles. Here,
Arthur Dent will not be able to meet aliens and have a generally
wild time until he has got hold of a babel fish:
>examine machine
The dispenser is tall, has a button at around eye-level, and says ‚Äė‚ÄėBabel Fish‚Äô‚Äô
in large letters. Anything dispensed would probably come out the slot at
around knee-level. It bears a small label which reads ‚Äė‚ÄėAnother fine product
of the Sirius Cybernetics Corporation.‚Äô‚Äô
>press dispenser button
A single babel fish shoots out of the slot. It sails across the room and through
a small hole in the wall, just under a metal hook.
>remove dressing gown
Okay, you‚Äôre no longer wearing your gown.
>hang gown on hook
The gown is now hanging from the hook, covering a tiny hole.
>push dispenser button
A single babel fish shoots out of the slot. It sails across the room and hits the
dressing gown. The fish slides down the sleeve of the gown and falls to the
floor, vanishing through the grating of a hitherto unnoticed drain.
>put towel over drain
The towel completely covers the drain.

(For the final solution, still some way off, see ‚ÄėThe Hitchhiker‚Äôs Guide To The
Galaxy‚Äô.) A good game mixes easy puzzles with hard, but no puzzle should
be so simple that one obvious command solves it. On the other hand nor
should its solution, once guessed, take ridiculously long to achieve, or require
endless repetition: such as to fetch something pointlessly distant, or to solve
Ôú≥ÔúłÔú≤

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
an eight-discs Tower of Hanoi, or to keep juggling objects so that only three
are carried at any one time. Here are two basic pitfalls:
By convention, every word or phrase in a
The ‚Äė‚ÄėGet‚ÄďX‚ÄďUse‚ÄďX‚Äô‚Äô syndrome.
cryptic crossword clue is used exactly once to account for some part of the
answer. The equivalent in adventure games is the equation ‚Äė‚Äėone object =
one puzzle solved‚Äô‚Äô, where the player picks up a bicycle pump and looks for a
bicycle, picks up a pin and looks for a balloon, and so on. Once used, an object
can be dropped, for it surely will not be needed again. But this convention
rapidly drains away realism, and most designers try to break the equation in
every way possible: with red herrings (one object = no solutions), collection
puzzles (many objects = one solution), multiple solutions (any of several
objects = one solution) and multiple usages (one object = many solutions).
The ‚Äė‚ÄėWhat‚Äôs-the-Verb‚Äô‚Äô syndrome.
In ‚ÄėBallyhoo‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėwhip lion‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėhit lion
with whip‚Äô‚Äô are inequivalent and only one of them does any taming. The
following, from ‚ÄėSorcerer‚Äô, can only be called a bug:
>unlock journal
(with the small key)
No spell would help with that!
>open journal
(with the small key)
The journal springs open.

(For a third example, the wording needed to use the brick in ‚ÄėZork II‚Äô is most
unfair.) In many games the ‚Äė‚Äėexamine‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėsearch‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚Äėlook inside‚Äô‚Äô verbs
all perform different actions, and it is easy to accidentally design a hidden
treasure in such a way that only one of these will find it. (Though at the other
extreme, excessive tolerance for verbs leads to everything being ‚Äė‚Äėmoved‚Äô‚Äô,
not ‚Äė‚Äėpushed‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėpulled‚Äô‚Äô or ‚Äė‚Äėrotated‚Äô‚Äô.) Similarly, in the ‚Äė‚ÄėWhat‚Äôs-the-Noun‚Äô‚Äô
syndrome, an object stubbornly fails to respond to reasonable synonyms, such
as ‚Äė‚Äėsword‚Äô‚Äô for ‚Äė‚Äėgladius‚Äô‚Äô or ‚Äė‚Äėfootball‚Äô‚Äô for a soccer ball. But perhaps a remark
on a sad subject might be intruded here. The Japanese woman near the start
of ‚ÄėTrinity‚Äô can be called ‚Äė‚Äėyellow‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚ÄėJap‚Äô‚Äô, terms with a grisly resonance.
The game shows nothing but respect for her: should it allow the player to do
otherwise?
Variety is valuable, but logic and fairness are paramount. ‚Äė‚ÄėIs the writer pulling
a rabbit out of a hat or do you see the fuzzy ears first?‚Äô‚Äô (Dave Lebling). Gareth
Rees suggests that one way to ensure that puzzles are consistent with the game
containing them is to write a sample transcript of play first:
Ôú≥ÔúłÔú≥

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
It stops me coding anything until I have a puzzle fairly well fleshed out in my
mind. Too often it‚Äôs tempting to start coding something one way and then
discover that later developments need a different approach.
It makes me think like a player (I try to . . . include a selection of the silly
things that I would be liable to type if I were playing the game . . .). Often
when coding it becomes habit just to fail to deal with situations and responses
that are tricky to write. Having them appear on the script forces me to say
to myself, ‚ÄėIt may be tough to code but it‚Äôll appear natural in the game and
that‚Äôs worth it‚Äô. I also find it hard to get into the habit of providing interesting
responses to failed actions, and the script helps with this.

Another approach is to chain backwards from a goal, repeatedly asking ‚Äė‚Äėhow
can I obstruct this further?‚Äô‚Äô, so that the plot line becomes, like a computer
drawing of a fractal curve, more crinkly with each iteration. Peter Killworth, in
his book How to Write Adventure Games for the BBC Microcomputer Model B and
Acorn Electron (whose opening words are ‚Äė‚ÄėAdventure games are like avocado
pears‚Äô‚Äô) describes an entire game this way (‚ÄėRoman‚Äô, 1984). Thus you need to
pay a debt to a Senator, so you need to steal a bust from a temple, but that
means impersonating a priest, by sacrificing a chicken with a gladius, which
means catching a chicken (you scare it with a cat, but the cat must be attracted
by a mouse, which you need to catch with a mousetrap): and the gladius isn‚Äôt
just lying around, either. You also need a torch, which
. . . needs soaking in oil first, just as candles need wax to burn. So we‚Äôd
better organise a pool of oil through which the player can walk . . . When the
player gets to a source of flame ‚Äď how about a brazier of coals, which will
have to be untakeable? ‚Äď he can attempt to light his torch. It isn‚Äôt oily, it
burns to a stump well-nigh instantly . . . If it is oily, it‚Äôll catch fire . . . No,
that‚Äôs too simple. If a player is soaked in oil too, he‚Äôll probably catch fire
too! . . . We‚Äôll create a damp, misty area, where the player is assassinated by
a runaway slave, unless he enters while on fire. Then he will be safe, because
the mist will condense on his body . . . So the poor player, staggering around
and on fire, will try the mist, but to his disappointment the torch will go
out permanently too! The solution is trivial ‚Äď he must drop the torch before
entering the mist.

(Killworth‚Äôs games are not known for their qualms and the player, it will be
noted, forfeits the game without any warning by lighting the unoily torch or
exploring the misty area.) This kind of plotting, with puzzles strung together
like beads onto a necklace, offers the considerable advantage of lending
coherency. But it is also liable to make long, linear sequences of puzzles which
must be completed in an exact order. ‚Äė‚ÄėI‚Äôve found it incredibly hard to keep
the puzzles from leading the whole story‚Äô‚Äô (David M. Baggett, 1994).
¬∑
Ôú≥ÔúłÔúī

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
In the TraiteŐĀ des systeŐÄmes of 1749, Condillac wrote: ‚Äė‚ÄėWhat could be
Mazes.
more ridiculous than that men awakening from a profound sleep, and finding
themselves in the middle of a labyrinth, should lay down general principles for
discovering the way out?‚Äô‚Äô Ridiculous, but very human, because the dogged
exploration of a maze is dull indeed, repetitious and irritatingly drawn-out: far
more enjoyment is to be found in the working-out of its general principles.
Be it clearly said: it is designers who like mazes (‚Äė‚ÄėConcocting such mazes
is one of my delights‚Äô‚Äô ‚Äď Peter Killworth); players do not like mazes. In the
original puzzle, a tangled set of rooms have indistinguishable descriptions
so that mapping becomes impossible: the original solution is to make the
rooms distinguishable again by littering them with objects as markers. This
solution is easily obstructed (the ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô pirate and the ‚ÄėZork I‚Äô thief wander
around picking the objects up again), but this only makes the experience more
tiresome. When David Baggett was asked ‚Äė‚ÄėHow do I make my maze so that it
doesn‚Äôt have the standard solution?‚Äô‚Äô, his entire reply was: ‚Äė‚ÄėOmit it.‚Äô‚Äô
Nevertheless, like the writing of locked-room mysteries, the devising of
new solutions for mazes ‚Äď usually involving guides, or hidden signs, or ways to
see ‚Äď is a modest art form in its own right: novelty being the essential point,
though it is equally important to signal to the player that a novel solution
exists. The unique The Adventure Gamer‚Äôs Manual (1992), by the Cornish
vicar and eccentric writer Bob Redrup, devotes all of Chapters 7 and 8 to
solving maze variants: a faintly weary tone is maintained throughout. Redrup
was an aficionado of the Topologika, and thus of the formerly Cambridge
University games, which are simply riddled with mazes. Here, in ‚ÄėCrobe‚Äô
(1986) by the indefatigable maze-maker Jonathan Partington, the player is
evidently not expected to explore haphazardly:
You are in an underground marsh, a treacherous place where everything
looks alike and water and slime lap around your feet. One false move would
mean death, but you do at least have the choice of 8 horizontal directions to
wander in.

Because lethal unless solved utterly, this is a benign sort of maze. Likewise, in
‚ÄėKingdom of Hamil‚Äô (1982):
You are in the Maze of Hamil. Light streams in through many gaps in the
rocks. There is the constant sound of rockfalls, distant and not-so-distant.
There is a small nickel hexagon here, with the inscription ‚Äė‚Äė1 PFENTIME‚Äô‚Äô.

Whatever is going on here, it doesn‚Äôt look like a simple matter of dropping
marker-objects. Partington also has a (thoroughly unfashionable) penchant
for elements of randomness which a player can overcome with great difficulty
by careful planning. The caryatid maze from ‚ÄėFyleet‚Äô (1985) makes the novel
twist of imposing random obstacles on a determined layout, and in ‚ÄėThe Quest
Ôú≥ÔúłÔúĶ

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
for the Sangraal‚Äô (1987): ‚Äė‚ÄėThere are exits in various directions, but, since the
island is rotating, these directions change continually.‚Äô‚Äô Infocom‚Äôs output has
its share of mazes, too, one per game: those in ‚ÄėStarcross‚Äô and the ‚ÄėEnchanter‚Äô,
‚ÄėSorcerer‚Äô, ‚ÄėSpellbreaker‚Äô trilogy are the most satisfying.
Light source puzzles.
Almost as disliked, but offering a little more scope to
the designer, is the ‚Äė‚Äėbringing light to darkness‚Äô‚Äô puzzle. The two standards
reduce to refilling a lamp with limited oil and bringing light to a dark room
which can apparently only be reached by a player who hasn‚Äôt got a light
source. (‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô includes both. The lake and aqueduct areas of ‚ÄėZork III‚Äô
have an elegant light puzzle, probably the best thing in an otherwise so-so
game.) Darkness need not be a problem to be solved, though: it might be a
fact of life. Though few games have tried this (but visit the secret passage in
‚ÄėChristminster‚Äô), a large permanently dark area might still be explored with the
other senses.
Capacity and exhaustion puzzles.
Again, unpopular because their solution
is normally tiresomely repetitive, forcing the player to keep putting things
down and picking them up again. It can seem ridiculous that the protagonist
can carry hundreds of bulky and fiddly things around all the time, so many
designers impose a limit for realism‚Äôs sake, typically of seven objects. It is
bad form to set puzzles making life difficult because the limit is four and
not five (after all, in emergency anyone can always carry one more item). In
some games the limit is instead on total weight. Taking realism further, some
games measure a state of health or even numerical levels of ‚Äė‚Äėstrength‚Äô‚Äô and
‚Äė‚Äėconstitution‚Äô‚Äô during play. The protagonist grows hungry and needs food,
tired and needs sleep (in ‚ÄėEnchanter‚Äô he is positively narcoleptic), wounded
and needs recuperation. ‚ÄėPlanetfall‚Äô simulated a progressive illness whose
symptoms are increasing need for food and sleep, and put many players off
this kind of puzzle for life. Exhaustion rules are difficult to make fair. A rule
requiring a return to an orchard for fruit should be watched carefully, as it will
irritate a player to have to do this for a second, a third or a tenth time.
Timed puzzles.
Completing this round-up of unpopular but still sometimes
justified puzzles are those which involve timed events, running along a script
which requires the player to do something specific at one particular moment.
In the prologue to ‚ÄėThe Hitchhiker‚Äôs Guide To The Galaxy‚Äô, why would any
player buy the cheese sandwich in the pub and then feed it to the dog in
the lane, on the one and only turn in which this is possible? Admittedly, an
alternative exists later on, but this is not evident at the time. Mike Roberts
(Usenet posting, 1999):
Ôú≥ÔúłÔú∂

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
Aside from the annoyance, the reason I try to avoid timed puzzles is that
they make you acutely aware that you‚Äôre playing a game. As soon as I get
into a save-try-restore loop, any sense of immersion is destroyed for me; I
instead feel like I‚Äôm debugging a program, since I‚Äôm running through a series
of inputs to figure out how the game responds.

This ‚Äė‚Äėsense of immersion‚Äô‚Äô can partly be restored by keying events not to game
turns but to the time of day, provided this fits the scenario, and making each
stage last for a great many more turns than are strictly needed to solve the
puzzles. Events which come only to he who waits are also problematic. In the
Land of Shadow of ‚ÄėZork III‚Äô, only the player who decides for some reason to
wait on a fairly uninteresting Ledge will be rewarded with a visitor. (This case
is defensible on grounds of context, but only just.)
A designer who wants players to think of some items as useful
Utility objects.
needs to provide many situations ‚Äď more than one, anyway ‚Äď in which they can
be used. A hallmark of better-designed games is that the player accumulates
a few useful tools during play and wants to keep them to hand thereafter.
(Cf. the crowbar and gloves in ‚ÄėThe Lurking Horror‚Äô.)
Keys and doors.
Almost all games close off segments of the map on a
temporary basis by putting them behind locked doors. Many variations on
this theme are extant in the literature: coded messages on the door, illusory
defences, gate-keepers, the key being in the lock on the wrong side and so on.
More usually a locked door signals to the player that a different puzzle (i.e.,
finding the key) has to be solved before this one can be, so that a designer
uses it to impose a chronology on events. Questions to ask here include: if
there are people just inside, do they react when the protagonist knocks on the
door, or tries to break it down or ram it? Can the door be opened, closed,
locked or unlocked from both sides? Are there skeleton or master-keys capable
of opening many different doors? Are the keys which do open different doors
sufficiently distinctive in appearance? Roger Giner-Sorolla commented that
keys are the most naked kind of Get‚ÄďX‚ÄďUse‚ÄďX puzzle:
One can only find so many keys inside fishes‚Äô bellies, lost in the wainscotting,
dropped at random in corridors, or hanging around guard dogs‚Äô necks before
the artifice of the puzzle structure becomes painfully clear. By contrast, all six
of the keys in ‚ÄėChristminster‚Äô are hidden in places where one might actually
keep a key, and all their locks are guarding places that one would expect to be
locked; moreover, we end the game with a pretty clear idea of who normally
uses each key and why.

Machinery and vehicles.
Machines are among the easiest puzzles to design:
they have levers or ropes to pull, switches to press, cogs to turn. They need not
Ôú≥ÔúłÔú∑

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
make conversation or respond to anything beyond their function. They often
require specialised tools, which brings in objects. They can transform the game
in a semi-magical way; time travel or transforming coal to diamond being the
clicheŐĀs. They can also connect together different locations: chains, swinging
arms and chutes may run across the map, and help to glue it together. Writing
in the TADS manual, Mike Roberts makes the useful point that machines
assist interactivity:
For example, you might design a machine, described as ‚Äė‚Äėa small metal box
with a button, a short plastic hose on one side, and a large metal pipe on
the other side.‚Äô‚Äô When the button is pushed, ‚Äė‚Äėa loud hissing comes from
the plastic hose for a moment, then a large drop of clear liquid drops out of
the pipe, which hits the floor and quickly evaporates into a white cloud of
vapor.‚Äô‚Äô If the player puts the plastic hose in a glass of water and the button
is pushed, ‚Äė‚Äėthe water is sucked into the plastic tube, and few moments later
a block of ice is dropped out of the pipe.‚Äô‚Äô This allows the player to learn
by experimentation what the machine does, which is more fun for the player
than if you had labelled the machine ‚Äė‚Äėa freezer‚Äô‚Äô or some such.

In machine puzzles, then, the player experiments with the controls and
forms a theory of what is happening. With larger machines this involves
visualising the physical construction of the components and how they affect
each other: ‚ÄėHollywood Hijinx‚Äô is a tour de force of such puzzles, with a
see-saw and a suspended safe. But the literature also includes highly complex
self-contained machines presenting something of a black box whose internals
must be deduced, such as a B-52 bomber and an Enigma cipher machine
(‚ÄėJigsaw‚Äô) and a computer which is programmable in the language Scheme
(‚ÄėLists and Lists‚Äô, Andrew Plotkin, 1996). Vehicles in games to date have
included cars, tractors, fork-lift trucks, boats, hot-air balloons, log flumes,
punts and elephant rides. Vehicles increase the realism of a landscape, by
making it more than a set of rules about walking. They nevertheless need a
little care to code: for instance, to disallow driving up ladders or through a
narrow crevice.
Fire.
The elements all tangle up code but add to the illusion. Fire has many
useful properties ‚Äď it makes light, it destroys things, it can cause explosions
and chemical reactions, it cooks food, it softens materials, it can be passed
from one object to another ‚Äď but in the end it spreads, whereas the game‚Äôs
understanding doesn‚Äôt. If the player is allowed to carry a naked flame, then
the game is likely to need a rule to tell it whether or not every other item is
flammable, and so on.
In any location where water is available, players will try drinking,
Water.
swimming, washing, diving. They will try to walk away with, indeed on,
Ôú≥ÔúłÔúł

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
the water. Liquids make poor objects, because they need to be carried in
some container yet can be poured from one to another, and because they are
endlessly divisible. ‚Äė‚ÄėSome water‚Äô‚Äô can easily be made into ‚Äė‚Äėsome water‚Äô‚Äô and
‚Äė‚Äėsome water‚Äô‚Äô. If there‚Äôs more than one liquid in the game, can they be mixed?
Pouring liquid over something is likely to make a mess of it: yet why should
it be impossible? And so on. The compromise solution is usually to have a
bottle with a capacity of, say, 5 units of water, which can be refilled in any
room where there is water and so that 1 unit is drunk at a time. The player
who tries to pour water over most things is simply admonished and told not to.
Implementing swimming, or being underwater, is a different order of difficulty
again, and many games agree with ‚ÄėParc‚Äô (John Rennie, 1983) that ‚Äė‚Äėsince you
cannot free yourself, and since you are by nature an air-breathing mammal I‚Äôm
afraid you drown!‚Äô‚Äô. (Level 9‚Äôs game ‚ÄėAdventure Quest‚Äô is rare in containing
a coherently worked out underwater section, though many games have the
odd turn‚Äôs-worth of diving. ‚ÄėJinxter‚Äô (Georgina Sinclair and Michael Bywater,
1987), also has an elaborate underwater section, with a seldom-discovered
shipwreck to boot.) What happens to objects being held? Can the protagonist
swim while wearing heavy clothes, or carrying many things? Is it possible to
dive?
Smoke and fog can obscure the scene, but puzzles involving air are
Air.
mainly about its absence. The lack of oxygen to breathe has featured in many
games, not always through being underwater: ‚ÄėZork I‚Äô and ‚ÄėSorcerer‚Äô share a
mine with poor air, ‚ÄėStarcross‚Äô and ‚ÄėTrinity‚Äô include locations in the vacuum of
space. A scuba mask, space helmet or some other kind of breathing apparatus
is called for. (Other gases simulated include helium, explosive hydrogen and
laughing gas.) Can the protagonist speak, or eat, or listen, or taste while
wearing this apparatus?
Earth.
Digging for buried treasure. . . the shovel can be found in just about
every traditional-style game and a good many others which ought to know
better besides. The problem is that the player may want to dig anywhere and
everywhere, which the game will probably not want to implement: to dig may
artificially create a new location, or a new map connection, or a new container
‚Äď the hole left in the ground, that is. (The prologue to ‚ÄėInfidel‚Äô, though the
least interesting part of the game from the point of view of playing, has a good
implementation of digging through sand.)

Ôú≥ÔúłÔúĻ

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
Vegetation fits into almost any landscape, and on the grounds
Plants.
of interactivity generally plays some part in the game, which is good for
variety, because people deal differently with plants from machines and people.
Undergrowth can be pulled away from something obscured, or useful plants
picked. Trees and creeping plants ought to be climbable: players nearly always
try.
Animals.
In many ways preferable to people, animals add a splash of colour,
and what would the Garden of Eden have been without elephants, rabbits,
leopards and guinea pigs? They move and behave in curious and obsessive
ways, displaying what human characteristics they like but not needing to react
to conversation or to show human curiosity or surprise at what happens.
This makes them much easier to design, but it doesn‚Äôt exempt them from
characterisation. It‚Äôs a little predictable to make the player feed an animal
into obedience and then get it do something. (The bird in ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô is nicely
characterised, in that it is frightened by the rusty iron rod with a star on one
end. ‚ÄėTrinity‚Äô is positively overrun with animal life, with some critics having
called its roadrunner the most important character.)
Many of the early adventure games included trolls, orcs and
Monsters.
dragons, or else Frankenstein‚Äôs Monster, Dracula and vampire bats: some,
like ‚ÄėZork I‚Äô, allow hack-and-slay combat in the style of a role-playing game
like Dungeons and Dragons. Others, like the heavily infested and therefore
somewhat repetitive ‚ÄėMurdac‚Äô (Jonathan Partington, 1982), base all their
puzzles on getting past or getting rid of things. ‚Äė‚ÄėGetting past‚Äô‚Äô occurs often
because most monster puzzles are no more than doors with the decoration of
slavering fangs. Even when monsters wander, they are generally dull because ‚Äď
being monsters ‚Äď they have no unpredictable behaviour. Whereas the capacious
underworld of the same author‚Äôs ‚ÄėKingdom of Hamil‚Äô houses a baby hexapod
(a what?) and a Conan Doyle-like Lost World of dinosaurs, which is much
more the thing.
People.
So dawns the sixth day of creation: we have the mountains, rivers,
plants and animals, but as yet no people. The nightmare of coding real
characters is illustrated well by one of Dave Lebling‚Äôs example bugs from
‚ÄėSuspect‚Äô (1984):
>show corpse to michael
Michael doesn‚Äôt appear interested.

The body is that of Veronica, Michael‚Äôs wife. Objects representing people often
take extensive code, perhaps five times that needed for even a complicated
room, because they may need to react to events, follow instructions (‚Äė‚Äėrobot, go

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
south‚Äô‚Äô), wander around the map and make conversation of sorts (the woman
selling bread-crumbs in ‚ÄėTrinity‚Äô, who plays only a minor role, can say over

stronger supporting cast, too:
‚ÄėChristminster‚Äô does an exceptionally good job of outlining Christabel‚Äôs role
as a woman by limiting her actions (she can‚Äôt enter chapel bareheaded) and
through . . . dialogue (the villains and the Master are condescending, while
young Edward sees her as a confidante).

(Roger Giner-Sorolla.) What distinguishes a character from, say, a spanner is
that it has an attitude to the protagonist. One model of this has the current
attitude as a position in a ‚Äė‚Äėmood maze‚Äô‚Äô, with different moods being like

(Setting out in the ‚Äė‚Äėfeed‚Äô‚Äô direction from Suspicious leads nowhere, as the

reactive and without memory, so most designers would want to conceal this
fact by adding spontaneous or even random behaviour, startling the player
from time to time. Or, of course, by simulating some memory of events.
Adam Cadre‚Äôs ‚ÄėVaricella‚Äô (1999) handles conversation using around 450 flag
variables to remember which questions have been asked and answered before.
It also reformulates conversation. Here, the protagonist Varicella ‚Äď as bad as

>ask sierra about king

>ask sierra about king

placing great reliance on conversation.

Ropes and chains.

Are notoriously troublesome to implement consistently:

[Someone will] say ‚Äė‚ÄėWell, I‚Äôve got this rope. . . how do I do a rope? It can be
in two rooms at once if you tie it to something and take the end with you,
and can you tie things up with it and drag them around with you?‚Äô‚Äô
Then we‚Äôll stop and think and say, ‚Äė‚ÄėYou don‚Äôt want to have a rope in your
game,‚Äô‚Äô and that makes it much easier for the new writers, you see.
My new game [‚ÄėThe Lurking Horror‚Äô] has a chain in it, and it‚Äôs even worse
than a rope in almost every respect you can imagine and it‚Äôs caused me no
end of horror. . . the number of bugs that have come in on this chain alone
would stack from here to there and back again.

(Dave Lebling again. But the chain puzzle in ‚ÄėLurking‚Äô is a masterstroke.) ‚ÄėThe
Meteor, The Stone and a Long Glass of Sherbet‚Äô has a rope solving several
puzzles whose source code runs to 300 lines of Inform, which is more than
the whole ‚Äė‚Äėlily pond‚Äô‚Äô region took: put another way, 5% of the entire code is
occupied describing the rope. There is also a long ladder which is nearly as
bad, and which the player is not allowed to tie the rope to.
Riddles.
Numerous games (‚ÄėBeyond Zork‚Äô, ‚ÄėThe Path to Fortune‚Äô (Jeff
Cassidy and C. E. Forman, 1995)) include sphinxes or talking doors which
pose riddles to passing strangers, and the writing of good riddles is an art form
in itself. But who put these puzzle-obsessed doorkeepers there, and why? The
knock-knock joke door in Irene Callaci‚Äôs ‚ÄėMother Loose‚Äô (1998) sits much
more happily, as the game is a wry mingling of nursery-rhyme stories for her
six-year-old granddaughter (and indeed for the rest of us).
Decipherment.
Perhaps the most abstract and, if done well, the most
satisfying of puzzles are those which present a system of coded messages,
clues to the meaning of which are scattered across the game. ‚ÄėInfidel‚Äô has
hieroglyphics. ‚ÄėEdifice‚Äô (Lucian Smith, 1997) requires the player to learn
the language of Nalian, a puzzle which won considerable plaudits from
players. But there are non-linguistic decipherments, too: in a sense the map
of ‚ÄėSpellbreaker‚Äô is itself a cipher. On a smaller scale, several Cambridge
University games contain tricky cipher puzzles not unrelated to recreational
mathematics. ‚ÄėAvon‚Äô, for instance, has a substitution code which is insoluble,
but which it is possible to make partial deductions about: just enough to solve
the problem at hand.

At least in one view of interactive fiction, clues are essential and the
Clues.
principle should be that an ideally perceptive player could win on his or her first
attempt, without recourse to saved games: in particular, without knowledge
of past lives or of future events. (The exact opposite, in fact, of what ‚ÄėBrand

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
X‚Äô does. The player begins in a shop containing an aqualung, a cushion, a
bunch of keys, a piece of sausage, a teabag and a sign declaring that ‚Äė‚Äėonly two
implements may be removed from this shop under penalty of death, so choose
carefully‚Äô‚Äô.) Here are three clues which did not carry:
(1) In ‚ÄėDungeon Adventure‚Äô, a pride of lions is carved over a doorway. Any
player walking through falls into a lethal pit. Did you miss the clue?‚Ä†
(2) The diamond maze in ‚ÄėZork II‚Äô is almost impossible to fathom unless (or
even if) you are familiar with a certain multiple-innings team sport played
only in America. In the words of even its designer: ‚Äė‚Äėalways annoyed
me. . . pretty lame.‚Äô‚Äô
(3) Almost every player of ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô has considered the rock marked Y2 to
be a decoy, emblematic of the mysterious cave. But it was meant as a
clue: on the cave maps used by Will Crowther‚Äôs group, ‚Äė‚ÄėY2‚Äô‚Äô denoted a
secondary cave entrance, which in a certain sense is what this location is.‚Ä°
(1) is a bad pun, (2) an unconscious assumption and (3) an in-joke. Games
that are entirely in-jokes, like the subgenre of college campus simulations (‚ÄėThe
Lurking Horror‚Äô features MIT, Infocom‚Äôs alma mater) are at least deliberately
so, but it is all too easy for designers to include familiar objects from their
own lives into any game, and unconsciously assume that the player shares this
familiarity. When that familiarity is needed to solve a puzzle, the game may
become unplayable.
Luck and accidental solutions.
Small chance variations add to the fun, but
only small ones. The thief in ‚ÄėZork I‚Äô seems to me to be just about right in
this respect, and similarly the spinning room in ‚ÄėZork II‚Äô, but a ten-ton weight
which falls down and kills the player at a certain point in half of all games would
simply irritate. A particular danger occurs with low-probability events, one or a
combination of which might destroy the player‚Äôs chances. For instance, in the
earliest edition of ‚ÄėAdventureland‚Äô, the bees have an 8% chance of suffocation
each turn carried in the bottle: one needs to carry them for 10 or 11 turns,
giving the bees only a 40% chance of surviving to their destination.
Even in a puzzle with no element of luck, many problems are solved by
accident or trial and error. (The notorious Bank of Zork puzzle in ‚ÄėZork II‚Äô
has been understood by almost nobody who solved it.) This is unsatisfying for
‚Ä† Pride comes before a fall.
‚Ä° Several Cambridge University games, written by mathematicians, refer to ‚Äė‚ÄėJ4‚Äô‚Äô:
‚ÄėAcheton‚Äô, for instance, has a ‚Äė‚ÄėJ4 room‚Äô‚Äô rather like the ‚Äė‚ÄėY2 rock room‚Äô‚Äô. The thenrecent construction of the previously only hypothetical group J4 had completed the
classification theorem for finite simple groups, and was a departmental triumph. The
sign ‚Äė‚Äė‚ąÉJ4 ‚Äô‚Äô remained above a doorframe for some years.

Ôú≥ÔúĻÔú≥

¬ßÔúĶÔúį ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ©ÔĚßÔĚģ ÔĚĮÔĚ¶ ÔĚįÔĚĶÔĚļÔĚļÔĚ¨ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥
both player and designer, and some games take steps to try to avoid it. The
gold-assaying puzzle in ‚ÄėSpellbreaker‚Äô is such that a shrewd strategy will always
succeed, but in principle even a random strategy might succeed. The game rigs
the odds to ensure that it never does.
Optional, partial and multiple solutions.
Most designers like to give two or
more different solutions to a few puzzles in a game: it seems more real, it
means that even a winning player hasn‚Äôt found all of the game‚Äôs secrets and it
makes a difficult puzzle easier. (There are seven ways to open the child-proof
medicine bottle in ‚ÄėCurses‚Äô.) Multiple solutions to the same puzzle need to
be equally valid. The designer should not think of one solution as the ‚Äė‚Äėreal‚Äô‚Äô
one, or allow another ‚Äė‚Äėshort cut‚Äô‚Äô one to skip critical plot events ‚Äď this would
short-change the player. On the other hand the designer must be relaxed about
the inevitability that some part of his golden prose will never be seen whichever
path the player takes. Most additional solutions are added in play-testing, but
here is Brian Moriarty on ‚ÄėWishbringer‚Äô (1985):
Most of the problems in the story have two or more solutions. The easy
way out is to use Wishbringer. If a beginner gets frustrated, he can whip out
the magic stone, mumble a wish and keep on playing. Experienced players
can search for one of the logical solutions ‚Äď a bit harder, perhaps, but more
satisfying. It‚Äôs possible to complete the story without using any of the stone‚Äôs
seven wishes. In fact, that‚Äôs the only way to earn the full 100 points.
The puzzles are highly interconnected. Once you start wishing your problems
away, it‚Äôs very hard to continue playing without relying more and more on
the magic stone. The impotence of idle wishing ‚Äď that‚Äôs the moral of
‚ÄėWishbringer‚Äô. All really good stories have a moral.

Analogous perhaps to the Wishbringer stone, ‚ÄėEnchanter‚Äô has a one-use-only
anti-magic spell. Although this solves one in particular of the more difficult
puzzles, to use it up so early forfeits the game, since it is needed later. If you
do fall into this trap, one of the ingenious dream sequences offers an oblique
warning:
You dream of climbing in an unfamiliar place. You seem to climb forever,
beyond reason. A fleeting hope arises in you, and you search furiously in
your spell book and possessions for something. After a moment, you become
frantic as you realize that you don‚Äôt have it! You bolt awake in a cold sweat.

Rewards.
What reward for solving a puzzle? One is obvious: the game state
advances a little towards its completion. But the player at the keyboard needs
a reward as well: that the game should offer something new to look at. The
white cubes in ‚ÄėSpellbreaker‚Äô, with the power to teleport the protagonist to new
areas, are far more alluring than, say, the ‚Äė‚Äėplatinum pyramid‚Äô‚Äô of ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô,
which is only a noun with a few points attached and opens up no further map.

‚Äė‚Äė[A puzzle] should be logical, according to the logic of the game‚Äôs universe. In a fantasy
game, a puzzle can rely on magic, but the magic must be consistent throughout the
game. A puzzle should be original in some way, not just a rehash of an earlier puzzle
with different objects.‚Äô‚Äô (Steve Meretzky). ‚Äė‚ÄėMy basic principle of designing puzzles is
that the player should always know what he‚Äôs trying to accomplish. Metaphorically,
a player should always be able to find a locked door before he finds the key‚Äô‚Äô (Mike
Roberts in the TADS manual). ‚Äė‚ÄėIn all cases, after a particularly arduous puzzle, reward
the player with a few simpler ones‚Äô‚Äô (C. E. Forman, XYZZYnews 1). ‚Äė‚ÄėErr on the side of
easy. (He said, waiting to be struck dead for hypocrisy.)‚Äô‚Äô (Andrew Plotkin). ‚Äė‚ÄėThere‚Äôs
definitely a difference between ‚Äėsatisfying‚Äô and ‚Äėpertinent‚Äô.‚Äô‚Äô (Lucian Smith. These last

The room description

When beginning to code a design, it is tempting to give rooms
temporary descriptions (‚Äė‚ÄėSlab room.‚Äô‚Äô ‚Äė‚ÄėCloister.‚Äô‚Äô), and leave the
writing for later. There is no more depressing point than facing
a pile of 50 room descriptions to write, all at once, and feeling
that one‚Äôs enthusiasm has altogether gone. (The same applies to making an
over-detailed design before doing any coding.) Besides, when testing the rooms
concerned, one has no feeling of what the game will look like except tatty, and
this is also depressing. Also, writing room descriptions forces the designer to
think about what the room is ultimately for. So most designers like to write a
few at a time, as coding goes on, but to write them properly: and edit later for
consistency and second thoughts.
In any room description there are usually one to three essentials to get
across, and the rest is better cut or relegated to text appearing only if the player
chooses to examine something in particular. Even the most tedious junctions
deserve description, however, and description is more than a list of exits. Here
is ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô at its most graceful:
Shell Room
You‚Äôre in a large room carved out of sedimentary rock. The floor and walls
are littered with bits of shells embedded in the stone. A shallow passage
proceeds downward, and a somewhat steeper one leads up. A low hands and
knees passage enters from the south.
In Limestone Passage
You are walking along a gently sloping north/south passage lined with oddly
shaped limestone formations.

Note the geology, the slight unevenness of the ground and the variation in the
size of the tunnels. Nothing happens here, but it seems a real place.
Flippant room descriptions are best avoided if they will be often revisited.
Subtler humour is more durable:
On the wall by the bed is a slightly curved, full-length mirror. You reflect
upon this for a while.

(From the Cambridge University game ‚ÄėXenophobia‚Äô (Jonathan Mestel, 1989).
This wording is also neat in that it applies equally well on the tenth visit to
a location as on the first, whereas text like ‚Äė‚ÄėAstonished to see a mirror, you
leap back. . .‚Äô‚Äô would not.) About once in a game an author can get away with
something like this:

Ôú≥ÔúĻÔú∂

¬ßÔúĶÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ≤ÔĚĮÔĚĮÔĚ≠ ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚ≤ÔĚ©ÔĚįÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚĮÔĚģ
Observation Room
Calvin Coolidge once described windows as ‚Äė‚Äėrectangles of glass.‚Äô‚Äô If so, he
may have been thinking about the window which fills the western wall of this
room. A tiny closet lies to the north. A sign is posted next to the stairs which
lead both upwards and downwards.

A characteristic piece of Steve Meretzky‚Ä† from ‚ÄėLeather Goddesses of Phobos‚Äô,
demonstrating the lengths one has to go to when faced with a relentlessly
ordinary junction-with-window. The sentence Meretzky is at pains to avoid
is ‚Äė‚ÄėYou can go up, down or north.‚Äô‚Äô With care it is even possible to remove
mention of a room‚Äôs exits altogether, but only if the information is presented
in some other way. For instance:
Dark Cave
Little light seeps into this muddy, bone-scattered cave and always you long
for fresh air. Strange bubbles, pulsing and shifting as if alive, hang upon the
rock at crazy, irregular angles.
Black crabs scuttle about your feet.
>south
The only exit is back out north to the sea-shore.

Here, the ‚Äė‚ÄėYou can‚Äôt go that way‚Äô‚Äô message for the room has taken up the
slack.
Experienced players know all of the various formulae used in room
descriptions by heart: ‚Äė‚ÄėYou‚Äôre in‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚ÄėYou are in‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚ÄėThis is‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚ÄėYou have come
to‚Äô‚Äô and so forth. This, perhaps, is why some designers prefer impersonal
room descriptions, not mentioning ‚Äė‚Äėyou‚Äô‚Äô unless to say something other than
the obvious fact of being present. Once into the text then, as in all writing,
vocabulary counts. If there is a tree, of what species? If a chair, of what style?
(‚ÄėCutthroats‚Äô (Mike Berlyn and Jerry Wolper, 1984) describes a cupboard of
no particular interest as a ‚Äė‚Äėlopsided wooden dresser‚Äô‚Äô for the sake of painting
the scene.) Room descriptions should not always describe static, fixed things,
should bring in senses other than sight and should not always be monochrome.
Plainness and repetition are to be avoided at almost any cost:
You‚Äôre on a winding drive outside a magnificent door. Exits are west to a
woodshed, upwards to a vine and in through a door. You can see a vine.
>west
‚Ä† But Meretzky was not always as corny as his reputation. Influenced by ‚ÄėSuspended‚Äô, which he had play-tested and later called ‚Äė‚Äėprobably the most interesting and
daringly different game Infocom ever did‚Äô‚Äô, he used a virtual reality theme to construct
almost the only work of early IF to contain serious political themes: ‚ÄėA Mind Forever
Voyaging‚Äô (1985).

Ôú≥ÔúĻÔú∑

¬ßÔúĶÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ≤ÔĚĮÔĚĮÔĚ≠ ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚ≤ÔĚ©ÔĚįÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚĮÔĚģ
You‚Äôre in a woodshed in the swamp. Exits are east to a winding drive and
west to a herb garden. You can see a candle and a woodpile.

(‚ÄėThe Price of Magik‚Äô, BBC Micro version ‚Äď in the Amiga version of the later
‚ÄėTime and Magik Trilogy‚Äô re-release, the winding drive description ran to
an eighty-word essay, and did away entirely with the mechanically-generated
‚Äė‚Äėexits are‚Äô‚Äô sentence.) So much for what is bad. The following, from ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô
again, is something much more dangerous: the mediocre room description.
Whirlpool Room
You are in a magnificent cavern with a rushing stream, which cascades over a
sparkling waterfall into a roaring whirlpool which disappears through a hole
in the floor. Passages exit to the south and west.

This seems a decent enough try, but no novelist would write like this. Each
important noun ‚Äď ‚Äė‚Äėcavern‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėstream‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėwaterfall‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėwhirlpool‚Äô‚Äô ‚Äď has its
own adjective ‚Äď ‚Äė‚Äėmagnificent‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėrushing‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėsparkling‚Äô‚Äô, ‚Äė‚Äėroaring‚Äô‚Äô. The two
‚Äė‚Äėwhich‚Äô‚Äô clauses in a row are a little unhappy. ‚Äė‚ÄėCascades‚Äô‚Äô is good, but does
a stream cascade ‚Äė‚Äėover‚Äô‚Äô a waterfall? Does a whirlpool itself disappear? The
‚Äė‚Äėhole in the floor‚Äô‚Äô seems incongruous. Surely it must be underwater, indeed
deep underwater? Come to that, the geography could be better used, which
would also help to place the whirlpool within the cave (is it in the middle? on
one edge?). And why ‚Äė‚ÄėWhirlpool Room‚Äô‚Äô, which sounds like one of the perks
of a health club? Here is a second draft:
Whirlpool Ledge
The path runs a quarter-circle from south to west around a broken ledge
of this funnel cavern. A waterfall drops out of the darkness, catching the
lamplight as it cascades into the basin. Rapid currents whip into a roaring
whirlpool below.

Even so, there is nothing man-made, nothing alive, no colour and besides it
seems to miss the essential feature of all the mountain water-caves I‚Äôve ever
been to, so let us add a second paragraph (with a line break, which is easier on
the eye):
Blue-green algae hangs in clusters from the old guard-railing, which has
almost rusted clean through in the frigid, soaking air.

The algae and the guard-rail offer possibilities. Perhaps there are frogs who
could eat insect-eggs in the algae, or perhaps the player might find a use for
iron oxide, and could scrape rust from the railing. Certainly the railing should
break if a rope is tied to it. Is it safe to dive in? Does the water have a hypnotic
effect on someone staring into it? Is there anything dry which would become
damp if the player brought it through here? Might there be a second ledge
higher up where the stream falls into the cave?
Ôú≥ÔúĻÔúł

¬ßÔúĶÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ≤ÔĚĮÔĚĮÔĚ≠ ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚ≤ÔĚ©ÔĚįÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚĮÔĚģ
4 Lack of variety comes in many forms. Brian Howarth‚Äôs eleven ‚Äė‚ÄėMysterious
Adventures‚Äô‚Äô games written for the Scott Adams game engine invent some interesting
milieux (‚ÄėFeasibility Experiment‚Äô (1982), with objects like ‚Äė‚ÄėVague Shapes‚Äô‚Äô, is worth
a look) but they are highly repetitive and difficult to tell apart. The main weakness of
‚ÄėEnchanter‚Äô is a sparse, location-heavy map, especially in the prologue, where many
rooms over-describe their neighbours. Slightly at odds with the traditional dungeon
elsewhere, ‚ÄėEnchanter‚Äô blends in horror tableaux: dead grass ‚Äė‚Äėseems to grip at your
feet‚Äô‚Äô, a demon statue ‚Äė‚Äėseems to reach towards you‚Äô‚Äô. There‚Äôs a lot of ‚Äė‚Äėseeming‚Äô‚Äô
motion, because the deserted, blasted landscape is largely static: ‚Äė‚Äėlistless waves barely
stir the flotsam and jetsam‚Äô‚Äô sums it up only too well. The outcome would have
been mediocre had the puzzles in the game not been exceptionally good, and a few of
the interior locations appealing. Here is a fine example of the interior room as vista,
overlooking a landscape and drawing together the whole game‚Äôs map:
Map Room
This room in the high tower appears to be a map room, with hundreds of
ancient maps covering the walls. A huge globe, made of gold, sits on a
pedestal in the center of the room. Through the tower windows can be seen
a vast forest stretching out to the northeast and the sea, covered in fog, to the
east and south. Stairs to the south lead to the bottom of the tower.

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

It is a vexed question just how much land occupies a single location. Usually a
location represents a single room, perhaps ten yards across at the most. Really
large chambers are usually given several locations, so that a ballroom might be
divided into corners with names like ‚Äė‚ÄėBallroom Northwest‚Äô‚Äô and ‚Äė‚ÄėBallroom
Southwest‚Äô‚Äô. The ‚Äė‚Äėhuge cave about 3,000 feet across‚Äô‚Äô of ‚ÄėAcheton‚Äô occupies
no less than 16 locations, which although it conveys a sense of space can also
seem repetitious and wasteful.
At the other extreme it is sometimes necessary for a single location to do
duty for a great swathe of ground, especially out of doors, where drawing the
map can leave one with the same frustration as the set-designer for a Wagnerian
opera: everything indistinct and without edges. ‚ÄėSpellbreaker‚Äô, under tight
constraints on locations, includes one-location meadows and volcanos. The
reverse position is taken by the distinctive and plausible ‚ÄėGateway to Karos‚Äô
(Derek Haslam, 1984). Locations are superimposed in a square map-like grid
onto the rivers, cliffs, forest and so forth of the island of Karos, so that each
location represents perhaps one square kilometer:
Eastlands
You are in a cluster of roofless, abandoned buildings, apparently part of an
ore-washing mill. A dry water-channel runs northward, and a path leads
west.

Ôú≥ÔúĻÔúĻ

¬ßÔúĶÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ≤ÔĚĮÔĚĮÔĚ≠ ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚ≤ÔĚ©ÔĚįÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚĮÔĚģ
A garden spade, well used but still strong and sharp, lies abandoned here.

(About a dozen neighbouring locations share the short name ‚Äė‚ÄėEastlands‚Äô‚Äô.)
A middle position between ‚ÄėSpellbreaker‚Äô and ‚ÄėGateway to Karos‚Äô is taken by
‚ÄėShe‚Äôs Got a Thing For a Spring‚Äô (1997), an evocation by the nature photographer Brent VanFossen of the mountains of northwest America. Almost
an interactive postcard, this thoroughly appealing game features elks, mooses,
eagles and so forth, but is equally vivid with terrain and vegetation:
Granite Canyon
You‚Äôre on a shelf overlooking a small canyon, apparently carved by the nearby
stream and 20 or 30 feet deep. A rocky path enters from the west beside
a tangle of blackberries, and dead ends at a ledge overlooking the stream
below. Above you, granite walls continue to rise, the pink stones a beautiful
contrast to the clear blue sky. An animal trail leads up, too steep to walk, but
you might be able to make it in a scramble.
Beside you stands a small tower supporting one end of a steel cable.
The end of a spruce branch is just barely visible deep inside the blackberries.

A single location can also substitute for an infinite expanse, such as the
Neverending Lane of ‚ÄėJinxter‚Äô.
Another consideration in outdoor locations is that the slow process of
sunrise and sunset ought to affect room descriptions. ‚ÄėChristminster‚Äô organises
time so as to keep the player indoors between seven and ten p.m. so that only
two states are needed, full day and full night. ‚ÄėA Mind Forever Voyaging‚Äô
derives most of its impact from its depiction of the same city decaying in
ten-year stages, as it rolls forward in history like H. G. Wells‚Äôs classic (and
classically filmed) novel Things To Come. The definitive shifting-description
game of recent years is Andrew Plotkin‚Äôs ‚ÄėA Change in the Weather‚Äô (1995):
You‚Äôre standing on a ledge, on a rather steep, overgrown hillface. Greenery
hides the stream below and the hilltop above, and the meadows and sky
beyond sweep away into the incandescent west.
You‚Äôre standing on a ledge, on a rather steep, overgrown hillface. Rain hides
the stream below and the hilltop above, and to the west is only dark.

Descriptions alter not just through time passing, but also because of differences
in perspective. Still the most remarkable example is the ‚ÄėSuspended‚Äô complex,
which a player in suspended animation controls through robots with different
sensory perceptions. Here is the same place from four points of view:
I‚Äôm in a large room which looks like the inside of a globe. The walls seem
sculptured with wiring, swirling around the room‚Äôs perimeter, leading into a
tall column. The column itself has a door on its face. Doorways lead to the
west, south, east and northeast.

ÔúīÔúįÔúį

¬ßÔúĶÔúĪ ÔĚīÔĚ®ÔĚ• ÔĚ≤ÔĚĮÔĚĮÔĚ≠ ÔĚ§ÔĚ•ÔĚ≥ÔĚ£ÔĚ≤ÔĚ©ÔĚįÔĚīÔĚ©ÔĚĮÔĚģ
Sonar indicates a large, spherical open area with a hollow column running
from floor to ceiling. The column reflects sonar evenly indicating no
distinguishing external characteristics.
All around me charges flow, shaped by the very nature of this room. The
electrons are being channeled into an electrical column, central to this
environment.
A small humming can be detected from a column which extends from floor
to ceiling.

Another device, used in the spy thriller ‚ÄėBorder Zone‚Äô (Marc Blank, 1987), is
to respond to directions not with a description of the new location but with a
response about how you got there:
>east
You open the door and walk out into the passageway. You scan the
passageway, noting guards at either end, machine guns poised at their sides.
You don‚Äôt remember them from the beginning of the trip, so you can only
suppose that security has been tightened in the search for the American
agent.
>look
Outside Your Compartment
You are standing in the passageway that runs along the length of the car. At
either end of the passageway, a guard is standing, machine gun poised at his
side. Right now, you‚Äôre standing outside your own compartment.

‚ÄĘ REFERENCES

Mike Berlyn (XYZZYnews 17) groups the issues here under the headings Size/Scope,
Ceilings, Floor/Ground, Walls, Lighting and Mood (‚Äė‚Äėa depressed person is not likely
to have yellow-and-red throw pillows‚Äô‚Äô: oh?). ‚ÄĘGerry Kevin Wilson offers three don‚Äôts
for room descriptions: ‚Äė‚Äė1. Don‚Äôt mention a player‚Äôs actions in a description. 2. Don‚Äôt
mention moveable objects in a description. 3. Don‚Äôt exceed one screenful of text in a
description.‚Äô‚Äô ‚ÄĘ‚Äė‚ÄėVery often, a map or the plan of a building can suggest a plot element
that no amount of abstract thought could generate.‚Äô‚Äô (Gil and Beryl Williamson, in
Computer Adventures ‚Äď The Secret Art.) ‚ÄĘWisest of all, perhaps: ‚Äė‚ÄėIt‚Äôs awful to sit down
and think, ‚ÄėI‚Äôve got to write fifty room descriptions today, and each one of them has to
be clear, crisp and vivid while conveying exactly the information I want it to convey‚Äô ‚Äô‚Äô
(Gareth Rees, Usenet posting, 7/6/94). Don‚Äôt write them all at once.

¬ß52

Finishing
‚Äė‚Äė[A game in alpha-testing has] on the order of 4,000 bugs. Maybe
fifty percent are spelling and punctuation errors, extra spaces,
missing blank lines, and so on. Maybe one percent are crashes.‚Äô‚Äô
‚Äė‚ÄėI try to make sure that the sun is in the right position, or if you‚Äôre
in outer space the sun and moon are where they‚Äôre supposed to
be ‚Äď stuff like that. . . The wooden beam [in ‚ÄėInfidel‚Äô] is described
as being a certain length and width, and I calculated that it would
have to weigh 500 pounds.‚Äô‚Äô
‚ÄĒ Max Buxton and Gary Brennan (Infocom play-testers), 1987

So the game is built: the wood is rough and splintered, but it‚Äôs
recognisably a game. There is still a good month‚Äôs work to do,
easier work if less creative, and beyond that a good deal of drudgery
to fix bug after bug after bug.‚Ä† The first post-design task is to sort
out the scoring system, usually awarding points out of some pleasingly round
number and dividing them into rankings. Here is ‚ÄėZork II‚Äô:
Beginner (0), Amateur Adventurer (40), Novice Adventurer (80), Junior
Adventurer (160), Adventurer (240), Master (320), Wizard (360), Master
Adventurer (400)

This is disappointingly bland, and a more pleasing tradition is to name ranks for
the player‚Äôs profession in the game ‚Äď so that an orchestral musician might begin
as Triangle and rise through Second Violinist to Conductor. (In ‚ÄėSherlock‚Äô,
the lowest rank ‚Äď corresponding to zero achievement ‚Äď is Chief Superintendent
of Scotland Yard.) Among the questions to ask are: will every winner of the
game necessarily score exactly 400 out of 400? (This can be difficult to arrange
if even small acts are scored.) Will everyone entering the end game already
‚Ä† Dave Lebling starts work in the morning: ‚Äė‚ÄėEven a cup of yummy coffee won‚Äôt
improve things when you see ‚Äėpage 1 of 12‚Äô on the first bug report form.‚Äô‚Äô Many goodnatured pieces about testing appear in Infocom‚Äôs publicity newspapers. At best, it was
enormous fun, and Liz Cyr-Jones‚Äôs testing department made a lively and exhilarating
summer job. But there were also tensions. The tester who wrote that 12-page form saw
it not as dismaying but as the proud result of a job well done. It could be frustrating
that the bugs would be fixed at random intervals, or not at all, while revised versions
of the game would sometimes arrive without any clear indication of what had altered.
Brian Moriarty redesigned great swathes of game at the last minute, and the fact that
he had always said he would did not make this any less maddening.

ÔúīÔúįÔú≤

¬ßÔúĶÔú≤ ÔĚ¶ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚ®ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚß
have a score of 360, and so have earned the title ‚Äė‚ÄėWizard‚Äô‚Äô? Will the rank
‚Äė‚ÄėAmateur‚Äô‚Äô correspond exactly to having got out of the prologue and into the
middle game?
Unless the scoring system is worked out and the game can pass its entire
transcript of the ‚Äė‚Äėwinning‚Äô‚Äô solution without crashing or giving absurd replies,
it is too soon to go into play-testing.
4

Scoring systems vary greatly. ‚ÄėAdventure Quest‚Äô is scored ‚Äė‚Äėout of about 6,000‚Äô‚Äô,
and exemplifies the pinball-machine-like tendency to offer points by 5s, 10s or 100s.
Other games feel that one puzzle is one point, or award percentages, and still others
frown on score altogether because ‚Äė‚Äėthat‚Äôs not how life works‚Äô‚Äô. In ‚ÄėMoonmist‚Äô, scores
are described thus: ‚Äė‚Äė[Well, so far you‚Äôve met Lord Jack and all of the guests, washed
up from your trip... but you haven‚Äôt found the hidden treasure nor enough evidence
nor identified the ghost!]‚Äô‚Äô. In ‚ÄėZork III‚Äô, the player‚Äôs ‚Äė‚Äėpotential‚Äô‚Äô is given out of 7,
corresponding to which of seven challenges have been encountered (so that a score of
7 does not mean the game is over). In ‚ÄėThe Lurking Horror‚Äô, 20 major puzzles are
awarded 5 points apiece for a maximum of 100: the 20th puzzle is to win the game.
In some ports of ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô 1 point is awarded for each room visited for the first time,
and 1 for never having saved the game, a mean trick, plus the infamous ‚Äė‚ÄėLast Lousy
Point‚Äô‚Äô, awarded without any clue for dropping a particular object in a particular place,
an irrelevant act achieving nothing. (People used to have to disassemble the mainframe
game to discover this.)

¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
During the writing and maintenance of ‚ÄėChristminster‚Äô, Gareth Rees kept a
log of all 475 modifications prompted by play-testers and players. This log is
archived with the game‚Äôs source code at ftp.gmd.de and makes an interesting
case study. 224 reports requested additional interactivity and responses, often
to reasonable but wrong guesses made by the player. A further 86 arose
from incorrect responses or inconsistencies, 32 from typographical errors
and 79 from mistakes in computer programming, for instance in the game‚Äôs
complicated algorithms to handle telephony and the mixing of liquids.
At every stage in writing an interactive fiction it is easy to lapse into the
habit of writing an uninteractive one. A designer who has written a linear
story and then introduced some puzzles may imagine that the literary style
and effect of the game comes from the text originally written, but that isn‚Äôt
altogether true: most of the player‚Äôs time at the keyboard is spent trying the
wrong thing, so most of the player‚Äôs experience of the game lies in how it deals
with wrong guesses. This means that it‚Äôs essential to respond to as many of
attempts as possible, acknowledging that the player has made honest attempts,
and so helping to form a sort of relationship:

ÔúīÔúįÔú≥

¬ßÔúĶÔú≤ ÔĚ¶ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚ®ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚß
In the aquarium is a baby sea-serpent who eyes you suspiciously. His scaly
body writhes about in the huge tank.
>take serpent
He takes you instead. *Uurrp!*

This is from ‚ÄėZork II‚Äô, a program which is at least twice the size of ‚ÄėAdvent‚Äô
in spite of implementing a much smaller design. Almost all of that disparity is
due to its generous stock of responses. Similarly, ‚ÄėZork I‚Äô contains possibly the
first examples of alternative solutions to puzzles (the cyclops can be defeated
in two different ways, as can the Loud Room). If a play-tester can think of a
reasonable solution to which the game does not respond, it is worth considering
a redesign of the puzzle to allow both solutions. Even if not, a response should
be made which acknowledges that the player has made a good guess.
¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

Bugs in interactive fiction are individually puny, yet daunting by their number,
like a column of army ants. Just as the ‚ÄėChristminster‚Äô log (see above) gives an
idea of routine testing, so Graeme Cree‚Äôs catalogue of bugs in released Infocom
games (at www.xyzzynews.com) shows what can slip through the most rigorous
testing regime. Here are some common types of bug:
‚ÄĘ Slips of punctuation, spelling or grammar: for instance, ‚Äė‚Äėa orange‚Äô‚Äô. Infocom‚Äôs
games are quite clean in this respect, in part because an experienced editor of
books, John Prince, proof-read their text. (The Inform compiler allows a designer
to extract all the text of a game for spell-checking.)
‚ÄĘ Rooms being dark when they ought to be light (which tends not to show if the
designer habitually carries a lamp when testing), or not changing their state of
light/darkness when they should: as for instance when a skylight opens, or when the
sun sets, or when the candles in ‚ÄėZork I‚Äô are blown out. In ‚ÄėSherlock‚Äô (21/871214)
an expert player can cross fog-bound London without a lamp, exploiting various
items and places which turn out to have (undescribed) light.
‚ÄĘ Secondary properties of an object neglected: such as a fish being marked as edible,
or a door as fixed in place.‚Ä† ‚ÄėStarcross‚Äô (15/820901) neglected to mark a light
beam as being inanimate, so that the player could get rid of it by typing ‚Äė‚Äėbeam,
go west‚Äô‚Äô.
‚ÄĘ Map connections missed or mismatching. In ‚ÄėSuspended‚Äô (8/840521), the corridor
northeast from East End to Alpha FC has no connection back southwest: the
designer simply forgot to make one.
‚ÄĘ Something which ought to happen only once being possible more than once: such
as the breaking of a window, or a character greeting the player as a stranger. The
only known bug in any release of ‚ÄėWishbringer‚Äô (68/850501) allowed the player
‚Ä† It wasn‚Äôt until the fifth proofs of ¬ß12 of this manual that any of us noticed that the
‚Äė‚Äėgreat stone slab‚Äô‚Äô altar of ‚ÄėRuins‚Äô had never been made static.

ÔúīÔúįÔúī

¬ßÔúĶÔú≤ ÔĚ¶ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚ®ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚß
to take back a spent gold coin and spend it again, gaining ever more points ad
infinitum.
‚ÄĘ Failing to properly revise the state of the game after a major event. In ‚ÄėDeadline‚Äô
(18/820311), Ms Dunbar was sometimes able to appear even after her death and,
indeed, to be present in the same room as her dead body.
‚ÄĘ Small illogicalities: messages such as ‚Äė‚ÄėThe ball bounces on the ground and returns
to your hand.‚Äô‚Äô in mid-air or while wading through a ford; or being able to swim
with a suit of armour on, or wave the coat you‚Äôre wearing, or eat while wearing a
gas mask. In ‚ÄėHollywood Hijinx‚Äô (37/861215), you can empty a bucket of water
while swimming underwater.
‚ÄĘ Failing to check that necessary objects are actually present when the player tries
by implication to use them. In ‚ÄėThe Witness‚Äô (22/840924), you can ‚Äė‚Äėget a drink‚Äô‚Äô
anywhere in the game.
‚ÄĘ Containers with surreal abilities: such as a purse which can hold a stepladder, or
a candle whose flame remains lit, or a book which can still be read even when
stowed away inside a rucksack. Infocom‚Äôs struggle with the ‚ÄėZork‚Äô container bug
has passed into legend. Numerous cases were attended to, but in all eleven releases
of ‚ÄėZork I‚Äô you can still put the raft in the coffin, and then the coffin in the raft,
causing both to vanish.
‚ÄĘ Forgotten names: for instance ‚ÄėPlanetfall‚Äô (20/830708) forgot to allow the player
to call the microbe monster ‚Äė‚Äėmicrobe‚Äô‚Äô.
‚ÄĘ Inadequate namings: in ‚ÄėDeadline‚Äô (19/820427) the bathroom door on the first
floor was impossible to open because the game called it simply ‚Äė‚Äėdoor‚Äô‚Äô, but with
a closet door in the same location, an ambiguity arose which nothing the player
could type would resolve.
‚ÄĘ Actual bugs in code intended to extend the game‚Äôs simulation of the world, and
especially with ropes and liquids: in ‚ÄėInfidel‚Äô (22/830916) filling the chalice when
it is already full results in the text ‚Äė‚ÄėThe silver chalice is filled with water. The
silver chalice is empty.‚Äô‚Äô

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

The days of play-testing are harrowing. Dave Lebling again, on ‚ÄėSuspect‚Äô:
>bartender, give me a drink
‚Äė‚ÄėSorry, I‚Äôve been hired to mix drinks and that‚Äôs all.‚Äô‚Äô
>dance with alicia
Which Alicia do you mean, Alicia or the overcoat?
Veronica‚Äôs body is slumped behind the desk, strangled with a lariat.
>talk to veronica
Veronica‚Äôs body is listening.

(‚Äė‚ÄėLittle bugs, you know? Things no one would notice. At this point the tester‚Äôs
job is fairly easy. The story is like a house of cards ‚Äď it looks pretty solid but
the slightest touch collapses it.‚Äô‚Äô)
ÔúīÔúįÔúĶ

¬ßÔúĶÔú≤ ÔĚ¶ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚ®ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚß
Good play-testers are worth their weight in gold. Their first contribution
is to try things in a systematically perverse way. To quote Michael Kinyon,
whose effect may be felt almost everywhere in the present author‚Äôs games,
A tester with a new verb is like a kid with a hammer; every problem seems
like a nail.

And here is Neil deMause, on one of his play-testers:
He has an odd compulsion, when he plays IF games, to close doors behind
him. It‚Äôs a bizarre fastidiousness, not even remotely useful for an IF player,
but I love him for it, because he has uncovered bugs in this way that I never
would have found.

Games substantially grow in play-testing, and come alive. Irene Callaci‚Äôs
acknowledgements could speak for all designers:
I thought perhaps beta testing might reveal a couple of odd, off-the-wall
commands that weren‚Äôt implemented, or maybe a typo here and there, or
possibly an adjective or two I had forgotten. Not! I wasn‚Äôt even close to
being finished, and I didn‚Äôt even know it. ‚ÄėMother Loose‚Äô grew from 151K
to 199K during the beta testing period alone. Looking back now, if I had
released ‚ÄėMother Loose‚Äô when I thought it was ready, I would have crawled
under a rock from embarrassment. Thank you, thank you, thank you to all
my beta testers.

More is true even than this: the play-tester is to interactive fiction as the editor
is to the novel, and should be credited and acknowledged as such. Major
regions of ‚ÄėCurses‚Äô and ‚ÄėJigsaw‚Äô were thrown out politely but firmly by my own
play-testers as being substandard or unsuitable.‚Ä† A radical response to the
play-tester‚Äôs doubts is almost always better than papering over cracks.
After a first pass by one or two play-testers, and a consequent redrafting
exercise, the game can go to beta testing at the hands of perhaps six or seven
volunteers, who come to it fresh and treat it more as an entertainment and
less as an unexploded bomb. (At one time Infocom used two phases of betatesting, sometimes involving as many as 200 volunteers, even after pre-alpha
and alpha-testing in house.) It is wise to insist on reports in writing or email, or
some concrete form, and to ask for a series of reports, one at a time, rather than
waiting a month for an epic list of bugs. It can be useful for play-testers to keep
transcripts of their sessions with the game, and send them verbatim, because
these transcripts are eloquent of how difficult or easy the puzzles are and which
wrong guesses are tried. In its debugging version, ‚ÄėJigsaw‚Äô provided a verb
called ‚Äė‚Äėbug‚Äô‚Äô purely to help players type comments into such a transcript:
‚Ä† Similarly, the published source code to ‚ÄėChristminster‚Äô contains ‚Äė‚Äėoffcuts‚Äô‚Äô such as
a pulley-and-rope puzzle in the clock tower.

ÔúīÔúįÔú∂

¬ßÔúĶÔú≤ ÔĚ¶ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚ©ÔĚ≥ÔĚ®ÔĚ©ÔĚģÔĚß
>bug Miss Shutes is known as he
Oh dear.
>bug The corn bread isn‚Äôt edible
Is that so?

It is worth keeping in touch with play-testers to ensure that they are not utterly
stuck because of a bug or an unreasonable puzzle, but it‚Äôs important to give no
hints unless they are asked for.
¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑

¬∑

¬∑

A game is never finished, only abandoned. There is always one more bug,
or one more message which could be improved, or one more wry response to
drop in. Debugging is a creative process, even beyond the initial release, and
games commonly have four to ten revisions in their first couple of years in play.
In the end, of course, the designer walks away. Almost all the pre-1990
designers cited in the bibliography are still alive, but few are still designing,
and they often speak of their games as something fun but belonging to another
time in their lives: something they feel faintly self-conscious about, perhaps,
something that they did years ago: when they were in high school, when their
kids were young, when they did a little testing for Infocom, when computers
were less visual, when it was the state of the art, when the Ph.D. was at an
impasse. But if twenty-five years is an epoch in computing, it is not a long
time in the history of art, and the early designers remain a presence in a genre
which is younger and less settled than sometimes appears. Once in a while
Scott Adams causes a frisson by throwing a remark into a newsgroup mostly
read by designers to whom he seems a historical figure coeval with Scott of the
Antarctic. Can such a man really have an email address?
In a recent radio broadcast (1999), Douglas Adams said that the great
enjoyability of working on his games with Infocom was having fun with a new
medium before it became an art form and had serious articles written about it.
(Which pretty much puts this chapter in its place.) But that original fascination
dies hard, and the first and happiest discovery of anyone researching into
interactive fiction is that designers past are only too pleased to be rediscovered,
and willing to go to great trouble ‚Äď hunting through archives, attics and
obsolete equipment ‚Äď to see that their games can be trodden again.
An adventure game can be one of the most satisfying of works to have
written: perhaps because one can always polish it a little further, perhaps
because it has hidden and secret possibilities. But perhaps too because
something is made as well as written: and once made can never be unmade, so
that there will always be a small brick building by the end of the road, and in
it there will always be keys, food, a bottle and the lamp.

openable
Can be opened or closed, unless locked.
pluralname This single object‚Äôs name is in the plural. For instance, an object called
‚Äė‚Äėseedless grapes‚Äô‚Äô should have pluralname set. The library will then use
the pronoun ‚Äė‚Äėthem‚Äô‚Äô and the indefinite article ‚Äė‚Äėsome‚Äô‚Äô automatically.
proper
Its short name is a proper noun, and never preceded by ‚Äė‚Äėthe‚Äô‚Äô or ‚Äė‚ÄėThe‚Äô‚Äô.
The player‚Äôs object must have this (so something changed into will be

taken; ‚Äė‚Äėyou are unable to‚Äô‚Äô pull, push, or turn it.

The player gets OBJECT_SCORE points for picking it up for the first time;
or, if a room, ROOM_SCORE points for visiting it for the first time.

‚Äė‚ÄėFixed in place‚Äô‚Äô if player tries to take, remove, pull, push or turn.

‚Äė‚ÄėThings can be put on top of it.‚Äô‚Äô Affects scope and light; object lists
recurse through it; a possession will give it a LetGo action if the player

its contents in separate paragraphs if it is itself listed. Note: an object
cannot be both a container and a supporter.

when_on or when_off in room descriptions.

Player can talk to this object in ‚Äė‚Äėthing, do this‚Äô‚Äô style. This is useful for
microphones and the like, when animate is inappropriate.
transparent ‚Äė‚ÄėContents are visible.‚Äô‚Äô Affects scope and light; a transparent container
is treated as if it were open for printing of contents.
visited
‚Äė‚ÄėHas been or is being visited by the player.‚Äô‚Äô Given to a room
immediately after a Look first happens there: if this room is scored then
ROOM_SCORE points are awarded. Affects whether room descriptions are

Temporary flag used by Inform internals, also available to outside routines; can be used to select items for some lists printed by WriteListFrom.

‚Äė‚ÄėItem of clothing being worn.‚Äô‚Äô Should only be an object being immediately carried by player. Affects inventories; doesn‚Äôt count towards
the limit of MAX_CARRIED; won‚Äôt be automatically put away into the
